   where $\ell(\cdot, \cdot)$ is a pointwise loss (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy).

4. **Validation Phase.** For each candidate hyperparameter configuration $\lambda_j \in \Lambda$, evaluate the validation error $E_{\text{val}}(\lambda_j)$. Pick the optimal one:
   $$\lambda^{*} = \arg\min_{\lambda_j \in \Lambda} \; E_{\text{val}}(\lambda_j)$$
   Then, optionally, **retrain** on $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}} \cup \mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$ using $\lambda^{*}$ to obtain the final model $f_{\hat{\theta}^{*}}$.

5. **Testing Phase.** Use $\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$ to compute the *single* final reported metric. The model must not be touched again after this step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stratified Splitting:** For classification tasks, the random split must preserve the class prior $P(Y = c)$ in each subset. This is called **stratification** and is essential for imbalanced datasets (e.g., fraud detection where positives are 0.1%).

---

## 2.2 Cross-Validation: The Validation-Set Shortage Problem

When the dataset is small, dedicating 20% to a validation set leaves too little data for training. The KTU-mandated solution is **$k$-Fold Cross-Validation (CV)**.

**Algorithm — $k$-Fold Cross-Validation:**

1. Shuffle $\mathcal{D}$ and partition it into $k$ equal, disjoint folds: $\mathcal{D}_1, \mathcal{D}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_k$.
2. For $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:
   - Train the model on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_i$.
   - Validate (i.e., compute the error) on the held-out fold $\mathcal{D}_i$.
3. Aggregate the $k$ validation errors.

Special cases widely tested in KTU exams:
- **Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV):** $k = N$. Unbiased but computationally expensive — variance of the estimator is high.
- **Stratified $k$-Fold:** preserves class proportions in every fold (mandatory for imbalanced data).
- **Repeated $k$-Fold:** runs the $k$-fold procedure $r$ times with different shuffles to reduce variance.

---

## 2.3 Bias–Variance Tradeoff (Connected Concept)

The validation/test error is governed by the classical decomposition:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] = \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2\!\big[\hat{f}(x)\big]}_{\text{systematic error}} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Var}\!\big[\hat{f}(x)\big]}_{\text{model sensitivity}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sigma^2_{\varepsilon}}_{\text{irreducible noise}}
$$

- Increasing model capacity (e.g., higher polynomial degree, deeper tree) **lowers bias** but **raises variance** and risks **overfitting** — which is precisely what the validation set detects.
- The validation curve (validation error vs. model complexity) typically has a **U-shape**, with the minimum at the optimal complexity.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk (Training Error) | $E_{\text{train}} = \dfrac{1}{N_t}\sum_{i=1}^{N_t} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | $N_t = \vert\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}\vert$ |
| 2 | Generalization Error (True) | $E_{\text{gen}} = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y)\sim P}\big[\ell(f_\theta(x), y)\big]$ | Computed over the *true* distribution $P$ — unknown in practice. |
| 3 | Test Error Estimate | $\hat{E}_{\text{test}} = \dfrac{1}{N_s}\sum_{i=1}^{N_s} \ell(f_{\hat{\theta}^*}(x_i), y_i)$ | $N_s = \vert\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}\vert$; **unbiased** for $E_{\text{gen}}$ if i.i.d. |
| 4 | $k$-Fold CV Error | $E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)} = \dfrac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i$ | Each $E_i$ is the error on fold $\mathcal{D}_i$. |
| 5 | LOOCV Limit | $E_{\text{LOOCV}} = \lim_{k \to N} E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)}$ | Special case of $k$-fold. |
| 6 | Bias–Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y-\hat f)^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}(\hat f) + \sigma^2_\varepsilon$ | $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ is the Bayes-irreducible error. |
| 7 | Standard Split Ratios | $60/20/20, \; 70/15/15, \; 80/10/10$ | $N_1 + N_2 + N_3 = N$ always. |
| 8 | Stratified Split Condition | $\dfrac{\#\{Y=c \text{ in } \mathcal{D}_j\}}{N_j} = \dfrac{\#\{Y=c \text{ in } \mathcal{D}\}}{N}, \;\forall j,c$ | For classification with class $c$. |
| 9 | Bootstrap Resampling | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \dfrac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}_b$ | $B$ = number of bootstrap samples; alternative to CV. |
| 10 | Hold-Out Variance (CV) | $\text{Var}(E_{\text{CV}}) \approx \dfrac{1}{k}\,\text{Var}(E_i)$ | Decreases as $k$ increases. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Train/Val/Test Split |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis (Cancer Detection)** | Train on hospital-A scans, validate on hospital-B scans, test on hospital-C scans → tests *domain shift* robustness. |
| **Autonomous Driving (Tesla / Waymo)** | Massive 90/5/5 split; test set contains rare "edge cases" (accidents, jaywalkers) to ensure safety. |
| **Speech Recognition (Google Assistant)** | $k$-Fold CV across dialects to tune acoustic model hyperparameters. |
| **Fraud Detection (Banks)** | **Stratified** splitting is mandatory because fraud rate is $\approx 0.1\%$. |
| **Recommender Systems (Netflix)** | Time-based split — train on past 6 months, validate on the next 2 weeks, test on the latest week. Avoids *temporal leakage*. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The $k$-Fold Cross-Validation Error Estimator

We will rigorously derive why the $k$-fold CV estimate is an *almost unbiased* estimator of the true generalization error.

### Step 1 — Setting up the partitions

Let the full dataset $\mathcal{D}$ contain $N$ i.i.d. samples from the true distribution $P(X,Y)$. Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $k$ folds of (approximately) equal size:

$$
N = k \cdot m, \quad m = \lfloor N/k \rfloor
$$

Each fold: $\mathcal{D}_i$ with $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$, and $\vert\mathcal{D}_i\vert = m$.

### Step 2 — Defining the $i$-th held-out error

In iteration $i$, the model $f_{\hat{\theta}_{-i}}$ is trained on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \mathcal{D}_i$ (size $(k-1)m$) and evaluated on the held-out fold $\mathcal{D}_i$:

$$
E_i \;=\; \frac{1}{m} \sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in \mathcal{D}_i} \ell\!\big(f_{\hat{\theta}_{-i}}(x_j),\, y_j\big)
$$

### Step 3 — Aggregating the fold errors

The $k$-fold CV estimate is the simple arithmetic mean of the $k$ held-out errors:

$$
E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)} \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i
$$

### Step 4 — Showing the estimator is unbiased (sketch)

Taking expectation over the random dataset draw $\mathcal{D} \sim P^N$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)}\right] \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[E_i\right]
$$

By the **i.i.d. symmetry** of the fold indices, every $E_i$ has the same expectation. Moreover, in each iteration, the model is trained on $(k-1)m$ samples and tested on $m$ held-out samples drawn i.i.d. from $P$:

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[E_i\right] \;=\; \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}^{(i)}, \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}^{(i)}}\!\left[\frac{1}{m}\sum_{(x,y)\in\mathcal{D}_i} \ell(f_{\hat{\theta}}(x), y)\right]
$$

In the limit as $N \to \infty$ (so $(k-1)m \to \infty$ and $m \to \infty$), the trained model $f_{\hat{\theta}}$ converges to the *Bayes-optimal* classifier, and each $E_i \to E_{\text{gen}}$. Hence:

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\!\left[E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)}\right] \;=\; E_{\text{gen}}
$$

This proves that **$k$-fold CV is an asymptotically unbiased estimator** of the generalization error. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Bias-Variance Decomposition of the Squared-Error Loss

For a regression task, we want to understand the expected prediction error of a model $\hat{f}(x)$ on a new point $x$, given training data $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$.

### Step 1 — Write the expected squared error

$$
\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}, y}\!\left[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right]
\;=\; \mathbb{E}_{y \mid x}\!\left[\big(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right]
$$

### Step 2 — Expand the square (using $a = y - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}]$ and $b = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - \hat{f}$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\big(y - \hat f(x)\big)^2 &= \big(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)] - \hat f(x)\big)^2 \\
&= \big(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]\big)^2 + \big(\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)] - \hat f(x)\big)^2 \\
&\quad + 2\big(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]\big)\big(\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)] - \hat f(x)\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Take expectations

The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}[\hat{f}(x) \mid x] = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ (a constant w.r.t. $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$). Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat f(x)\big)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}_{y\mid x}\!\left[\big(y - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]\big)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}\!\left[\big(\hat f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]\big)^2\right] \\
&= \underbrace{\big(\mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)] - f^{*}(x)\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(\hat f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat f(x)]\big)^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2_\varepsilon}_{\text{Noise}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $f^{*}(x) = \mathbb{E}[y \mid x]$ is the *regression function* (Bayes predictor), and the noise variance is:

$$
\sigma^2_\varepsilon = \mathbb{E}_{y \mid x}\!\left[\big(y - f^{*}(x)\big)^2\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Final identity

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat f(x)\big)^2\right] \;=\; \text{Bias}^2\!\big(\hat f(x)\big) \;+\; \text{Var}\!\big(\hat f(x)\big) \;+\; \sigma^2_\varepsilon\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for the KTU Exam Hall:**
> - **Bias** = systematic mistake (model too simple → **underfitting**). Detected by *both* training and validation error being high.
> - **Variance** = model instability (model too complex → **overfitting**). Detected by *training error low* but *validation error high*.
> - **Noise** = Bayes floor. No model can beat it.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Why Test Set is Single-Use

Suppose we repeatedly use the test set to refine a model. Let the reported test error after $t$ such refinements be $E_{\text{test}}^{(t)}$. By the **multiple-comparisons argument**, the minimum over $T$ random estimates follows:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\min_{1 \le t \le T} E_{\text{test}}^{(t)}\right] \;\le\; E_{\text{gen}} - c \sqrt{\frac{\log T}{N_s}}
$$

Hence, as $T \to \infty$, the optimistic bias grows (towards $-\infty$ in the worst case), **invalidating** the test set. This is why a clean, untouched test set is sacred.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Table

| Criterion | Hold-Out (60/20/20) | $k$-Fold CV | LOOCV | Bootstrap |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bias of $E_{\text{gen}}$ estimate | Slightly biased (less data for training) | Almost unbiased | Unbiased | Slightly biased |
| Variance of estimate | High | Moderate | **High** (correlated folds) | Low |
| Computational Cost | Low ($1$ training) | Moderate ($k$ trainings) | **Very high** ($N$ trainings) | Moderate |
| Best Use Case | Large $N$ | Small–medium $N$ | Very small $N$ | Variance estimation |
| KTU Frequency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The End-to-End Train/Validate/Test Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Raw Dataset D with N samples"] --> B["Step 1 Shuffle and Stratify"]
    B --> C1["Training Set D_train 60 percent"]
    B --> C2["Validation Set D_val 20 percent"]
    B --> C3["Test Set D_test 20 percent"]
    C1 --> D["Step 2 Train Model f_theta on D_train"]
    D --> E["Step 3 Compute E_val on D_val"]
    E --> F{"Step 4 E_val acceptable?"}
    F -- "No" --> G["Tune Hyperparameters lambda"]
    G --> D
    F -- "Yes" --> H["Step 5 Retrain on D_train plus D_val"]
    H --> I["Step 6 Final Evaluation on D_test"]
    I --> J["Step 7 Report E_test ONCE and stop"]
    J --> K["Model f_theta_star is deployed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The loop `G → D` represents the *hyperparameter search* (grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization). Once `F → Yes`, the model is **retrained** on the combined train+val data to leverage the maximum number of samples for the final deployed model. The test set is touched **only once**.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — $k$-Fold Cross-Validation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Full Dataset D with N samples"] --> Shuffle["Shuffle D randomly"]
    Shuffle --> Split["Partition into k equal folds D1 to Dk"]
    Split --> LoopStart["For i in 1 to k"]
    LoopStart --> TrainI["Train on D minus Di"]
    TrainI --> ValI["Validate on Di and get error Ei"]
    ValI --> NextI{"i equals k?"}
    NextI -- "No" --> LoopStart
    NextI -- "Yes" --> Agg["Aggregate E_CV = mean of E1 to Ek"]
    Agg --> PickBest["Pick hyperparameter lambda that minimises E_CV"]
    PickBest --> Retrain["Retrain on full D with lambda star"]
    Retrain --> Test["Final test on held-out D_test"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Bias–Variance Tradeoff Diagnostic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Underfit["Underfitting Region"]
        U1["Bias is HIGH"] --> U2["Variance is LOW"] --> U3["Both train and val error are HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph GoodFit["Sweet Spot"]
        G1["Bias is LOW"] --> G2["Variance is LOW"] --> G3["Train error LOW, Val error LOW gap is small"]
    end
    subgraph Overfit["Overfitting Region"]
        O1["Bias is VERY LOW"] --> O2["Variance is HIGH"] --> O3["Train error NEAR ZERO, Val error HIGH gap is large"]
    end
    Underfit --> GoodFit
    GoodFit --> Overfit
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this diagram in your answer:** Plot the *training error* and *validation error* as functions of model complexity (or epoch number for neural networks). The optimal model lies at the **intersection point** of the rising validation curve and the falling training curve — this is the operationally-meaningful version of "low bias + low variance."

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Data Leakage Anti-Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Correct Pipeline"] --> A1["Train gets 60 percent"]
    A --> A2["Val gets 20 percent"]
    A --> A3["Test gets 20 percent"]
    A1 --> X1["Fit model parameters theta"]
    A2 --> X2["Tune hyperparameters lambda only"]
    A3 --> X3["Evaluate ONCE at the end"]

    B["Wrong Pipeline Data Leakage"] --> B1["Train gets 60 percent"]
    B --> B2["Val gets 20 percent"]
    B --> B3["Test gets 20 percent"]
    B1 --> Y1["Fit theta"]
    B2 --> Y2["Tune lambda"]
    B3 --> Y3["Evaluate"]
    Y3 --> Y4["Re-tune lambda using test"]
    Y4 --> Y5["Reported E_test is now BIASED and INVALID"]
    Y4 --> Y6["Marks deducted in KTU valuation"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up "validation" and "test" sets** in definitions. (KTU repeatedly tests this; a single swap = **−2 marks**.)
> 2. **Forgetting the i.i.d. assumption** when discussing the unbiasedness of test error. (Lose 1 mark if you say "test error is always equal to generalization error.")
> 3. **Writing "cross-validation removes overfitting"** — wrong. CV *detects* overfitting, it does not *eliminate* it.
> 4. **Not mentioning stratification** in imbalanced classification problems. (Lose 1 mark in any 7-mark sub-question on classification splits.)
> 5. **Confusing the bias term in Bias² + Var + σ² with statistical bias of an estimator.** These are *different* "biases" — be precise.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the roles of the training set, validation set, and test set in a machine learning pipeline.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Set | Role | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Training Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$) | The model **learns its parameters** $\theta$ (weights and biases) by minimizing the empirical loss over this subset. | Parameter estimation. |
| **Validation Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$) | The model **tunes its hyperparameters** $\lambda$ (e.g., regularization strength, tree depth, learning rate) by evaluating candidate models on this subset. | Hyperparameter tuning & model selection. |
| **Test Set** ($\mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}$) | A **completely held-out subset** touched only once at the end to provide an **unbiased estimate** of the generalization error $E_{\text{gen}}$. | Final performance reporting. |

The three subsets are mutually disjoint and together cover the full dataset $\mathcal{D}$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is $k$-fold cross-validation? Why is it preferred over a simple hold-out validation when the dataset is small?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In $k$-fold cross-validation, the dataset is partitioned into $k$ equal, disjoint folds. The model is trained $k$ times — each time using $k-1$ folds for training and the remaining one fold for validation. The final cross-validation error is the average of the $k$ held-out errors:

$$
E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i
$$

**Why preferred over hold-out for small datasets:**
1. **Less data wastage:** Every sample is used for validation exactly once and for training $k-1$ times, so no data is "wasted" in a single fixed hold-out fold.
2. **Reduced variance of the error estimate:** Averaging over $k$ folds gives a more stable estimate than a single 80/20 split, whose result depends heavily on *which* 20% was chosen.
3. **Almost unbiased estimate of $E_{\text{gen}}$** even with limited data, since each training fold uses $\frac{k-1}{k} \cdot N$ samples — close to the full $N$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the bias-variance tradeoff in machine learning with a suitable decomposition. Show that the expected prediction error can be decomposed into bias², variance, and irreducible noise terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary classification problem with a dataset of $N = 1000$ samples where 5% belong to the positive class, describe the procedure for a **stratified 5-fold cross-validation**. Why is stratification mandatory here? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the regression function.**

For a regression task, define the *true* regression function as the conditional expectation of the target given the input:

$$
f^{*}(x) = \mathbb{E}[y \mid x]
$$

**Step 2 — Define bias and variance of an estimator.**

For a model $\hat{f}(x)$ learned from training data $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$:

$$
\text{Bias}\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}\!\big[\hat{f}(x)\big] - f^{*}(x)
$$

$$
\text{Var}\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}\!\left[\big(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the expected squared error.**

Starting from the expected prediction error at a fixed $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - f^{*} + f^{*} - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - f^{*}\big)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(f^{*} - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] + 2\,\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - f^{*}\big)\big(f^{*} - \hat{f}(x)\big)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[y \mid x] = f^{*}(x)$. Expanding the second term using $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] = m$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(f^{*} - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] = \big(f^{*} - m\big)^2 + \mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(\hat{f}(x) - m\big)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}
$$

**Step 4 — Final decomposition.**

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] = \text{Bias}^2\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) + \text{Var}\big(\hat{f}(x)\big) + \sigma^2_\varepsilon\;}
$$

where $\sigma^2_\varepsilon = \mathbb{E}[(y - f^*(x))^2]$ is the irreducible noise.

**Interpretation:**
- **High bias** (underfitting) → model is too simple, e.g., linear regression on a quadratic dataset.
- **High variance** (overfitting) → model is too complex, e.g., degree-50 polynomial on 30 points.
- The validation set detects *which regime* we are in by comparing train and validation error curves.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the class imbalance problem.**

Total samples $N = 1000$. Positive class: $1000 \times 0.05 = 50$ samples. Negative class: $950$ samples. Random splitting may produce folds with **zero** positive samples — making evaluation meaningless.

**Step 2 — Stratified 5-fold cross-validation procedure.**

1. Shuffle the dataset.
2. Partition into $k = 5$ folds, each of size $m = 200$.
3. **Stratification constraint:** Each fold must contain exactly $5\% \times 200 = 10$ positive and $90\% \times 200 = 190$ negative samples.
4. For $i = 1$ to $5$:
   - Train on the 4 folds (1600 samples — 40 positives, 760 negatives).
   - Validate on fold $i$ (200 samples — 10 positives, 190 negatives).
   - Compute a classification metric, e.g., F1-score: $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$.
5. Aggregate: $F_{1,\text{CV}} = \dfrac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} F_{1,i}$.

**Step 3 — Why stratification is mandatory.**

Without stratification, the variance of the cross-validation estimate explodes:
- Random chance could place only 2 or 3 positives in some folds and 15 in others.
- Metric values like F1-score or AUC become **highly variable** and unreliable.
- Stratification ensures the **class prior** $P(Y = \text{positive}) = 0.05$ is preserved in *every* fold, making the estimate **low-variance and reproducible**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of a simple hold-out (train/test) split. How does $k$-fold cross-validation address these limitations? Derive the expression for the $k$-fold cross-validation error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A startup is building a sentiment classifier for movie reviews. They have $N = 500$ labelled reviews and a candidate Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a hyperparameter $C \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$. Design a complete **train–validation–test protocol** with proper justification for the choice of split ratios and evaluation strategy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Limitations of hold-out split.**

1. **High variance of the error estimate:** The single 80/20 (or 70/30) split depends on *which* 20% was randomly chosen. Two different runs can give noticeably different test accuracies.
2. **Data wastage:** 20% of data is *only* used for testing and never for training. With small $N$, this loss is significant.
3. **No model selection capability:** A simple train/test split cannot be used to tune hyperparameters without contaminating the test set.
4. **No measure of model stability:** A single test score tells us nothing about how the model would behave on slightly different data.

**Step 2 — How $k$-fold CV addresses these.**

1. **Lower variance:** Averaging $k$ held-out errors reduces variance by a factor of $k$ (in the ideal i.i.d. case).
2. **Full data utilization:** Every sample participates in training $k-1$ times and validation once.
3. **Built-in model selection:** Pick the hyperparameter $\lambda$ that minimizes $E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)}$.
4. **Stability measure:** The standard deviation of the $k$ fold errors $\sigma_{\text{CV}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1}\sum_i (E_i - E_{\text{CV}})^2}$ quantifies model stability.

**Step 3 — Derivation of $k$-fold CV error.**

Partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $k$ disjoint folds $\{\mathcal{D}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{D}_k\}$, each of size $m$. For fold $i$:

$$
E_i = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{(x_j, y_j) \in \mathcal{D}_i} \ell\big(f_{\hat\theta_{-i}}(x_j), y_j\big)
$$

The $k$-fold cross-validation estimator is the sample mean:

$$
\boxed{\;E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i\;}
$$

This is an *almost unbiased* estimator of the true generalization error $E_{\text{gen}}$, with bias $\mathcal{O}(1/k)$ that vanishes as $k \to N$ (LOOCV limit).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Choose the split ratios.**

Given the small dataset size $N = 500$, a simple 80/20 train/test split would leave only 400 samples for training, which is suboptimal. We use a **3-way split with nested cross-validation**:

- **Outer split (Train+Val vs. Test):** 80% / 20% → 400 / 100 samples.
- **Inner split (Train vs. Val):** Use **5-fold CV** on the 400 training+val samples for hyperparameter tuning.

**Final protocol:**

| Phase | Data Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Inner CV (5-fold on 400 samples) | 320 train, 80 val per fold | Select best $C$ from $\{0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$. |
| 2. Refit on 400 samples with best $C$ | 400 training samples | Train final model. |
| 3. Evaluate once on 100 held-out samples | 100 test samples | Report final accuracy + std. dev. |

**Step 2 — Justify the choices.**

1. **Why 5-fold and not 10-fold?** With $N = 500$, 10-fold gives only 50 samples per validation fold, increasing variance. 5-fold gives 80 samples per fold, a good balance.
2. **Why not LOOCV?** $N = 500$ trainings is computationally expensive for SVM, and the variance of LOOCV is high.
3. **Stratification:** Movie reviews may be imbalanced (e.g., 60% positive, 40% negative) → use `StratifiedKFold`.
4. **Metric choice:** Use F1-score (not accuracy) if class imbalance is suspected; otherwise accuracy suffices.
5. **Final test set is touched ONCE** — no retraining after seeing test performance.

**Step 3 — Report.**

$$
\text{Final F1} = \frac{1}{100}\sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{test}}} F_1\big(f_{\hat\theta^*}(x_i), y_i\big)
$$

with a 95% confidence interval $\text{F1} \pm 1.96 \cdot \hat{\sigma}_{\text{F1}} / \sqrt{100}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (for the last 10 minutes before ESE):**

- ✅ **Three sets, three roles:** Train = fit $\theta$; Val = tune $\lambda$; Test = report once.
- ✅ **Splits are mutually disjoint** and together cover $\mathcal{D}$.
- ✅ **Standard ratios:** 60/20/20 (small $N$), 80/10/10 (large $N$), 90/5/5 (deep learning at scale).
- ✅ **$k$-fold CV error:** $E_{\text{CV}}^{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} E_i$ — almost unbiased estimator of $E_{\text{gen}}$.
- ✅ **LOOCV** = $k = N$ — unbiased but high variance; rare in practice for large $N$.
- ✅ **Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2_\varepsilon$** = total expected prediction error.
- ✅ **High bias = underfitting** (both train and val errors high).
- ✅ **High variance = overfitting** (train error low, val error high).
- ✅ **Stratification** is mandatory for imbalanced classification.
- ✅ **Test set is sacred** — touch it ONCE, after all model selection is finished.
- ✅ **Time-based split** (not random) for time-series data to avoid temporal leakage.
- ✅ **Cross-validation detects overfitting** but does **not** eliminate it.
- ✅ **The reported test error is an unbiased estimate** of $E_{\text{gen}}$ **only if** the test set was never used in any decision-making step.
- ✅ **Information leakage** = using any information from the test set during training → invalidates the test error.
- ✅ **Hyperparameter** ≠ **parameter**: hyperparameters are *not* learned by gradient descent; they are *chosen* by validation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation Measures for Classification - Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve, Area Under Curve (AUC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Evaluation Measures for Classification - Precision, Recall, Accuracy, F-Measure, Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve, Area Under Curve (AUC) # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Confusion Matrix: The Foundation of All Classification Metrics Before any classification metric can be computed, we require a structured summary of prediction outcomes called the **Confusion Matrix** (also called the *Error Matrix* or *Contingency Table*). Every metric in this module is derived directly from its four fundamental cells. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *The Confusion Matrix is a tabular visualization of the performance of a classification model in which the rows represent the actual (true) classes and the columns represent the predicted classes, or vice versa. Each cell counts the number of instances belonging to that combination of true and predicted labels.* | Cell | Symbol | Meaning | |------|--------|---------| | True Positive | **TP** | Model predicted **Positive**, and the truth is **Positive** ✅ | | True Negative | **TN** | Model predicted **Negative**, and the truth is **Negative** ✅ | | False Positive | **FP** | Model predicted **Positive**, but the truth is **Negative** ❌ *(Type I Error)* | | False Negative | **FN** | Model predicted **Negative**, but the truth is **Positive** ❌ *(Type II Error)* | ### 🌟 Real-World Analogy: The Airport Security Analogy Imagine you are a **security officer** at an airport with a metal detector: - **TP (True Positive):** A real smuggler is correctly detected. 👮‍♂️✋🧳 - **TN (True Negative):** A normal traveler is correctly allowed through. 👮‍♂️✅🧳 - **FP (False Positive):** An innocent traveler is wrongly flagged (false alarm). 👮‍♂️🚨😱 - **FN (False Negative):** A real smuggler sneaks past undetected (missed threat). 👮‍♂️😴💼 In medical diagnosis, an **FN** is far more dangerous (a cancer patient told they are healthy) than an **FP** (a healthy person told to take more tests). This single insight explains why no one metric is sufficient — context dictates the choice. --- ## 1.2 Accuracy > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *Accuracy is the ratio of correctly predicted observations (both Positives and Negatives) to the total number of observations.* $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$ **Intuition:** Out of everyone who came to the airport, how many were correctly cleared or correctly stopped? It is the simplest "hit rate" of the model. **⚠️ KTU Pitfall:** Accuracy is **misleading on imbalanced datasets**. If 99 out of 100 emails are not spam, a dumb model that always says "not spam" achieves 99% accuracy but is useless. --- ## 1.3 Precision (Positive Predictive Value) > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *Precision measures the proportion of correct Positive predictions out of all Positive predictions made by the model.* $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ **Intuition (Airport Analogy):** Of all the travelers the system **flagged as suspicious**, how many were **actually** smugglers? High precision = few false alarms. **Engineering Use Case:** Spam filters, recommendation systems — anywhere false positives are costly or annoying. --- ## 1.4 Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate / Hit Rate) > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *Recall (also called Sensitivity or TPR) measures the proportion of actual Positives that were correctly identified by the model.* $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ **Intuition:** Of all the **real smugglers** who walked past, how many did the system actually catch? High recall = few missed threats. **Engineering Use Case:** Cancer detection, fraud detection, security screening — where missing a positive is catastrophic. --- ## 1.5 F-Measure (F1-Score and Fβ-Score) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** *The F-Measure is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single scalar that balances both metrics — especially useful when the class distribution is uneven.* $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ **Intuition:** Harmonic mean (not arithmetic) punishes extreme values. A model with 100% precision but 0% recall gets an F1 of 0, not 50. **General Fβ-Score:** Adjusts the weight of Recall relative to Precision. $$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ - **β = 1:** Equal weight to both (standard F1). - **β > 1:** Recall is weighted more (e.g., F2 for medical screening). - **β < 1:** Precision is weighted more (e.g., F0.5 for spam filtering). --- ## 1.6 Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** *The ROC Curve is a two-dimensional graphical plot that illustrates the diagnostic ability of a binary classifier as its discrimination threshold is varied. It plots the True Positive Rate (TPR) on the y-axis against the False Positive Rate (FPR) on the x-axis.* **Intuition:** The ROC curve is a **threshold-independent** measure. Instead of asking "how good is the model at one threshold?", it asks "how well can the model **separate** the two classes across all possible thresholds?" --- ## 1.7 Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** *AUC represents the probability that the classifier will rank a randomly chosen Positive instance higher than a randomly chosen Negative instance. It is a scalar summary of the ROC curve ranging from 0.0 to 1.0.* **Intuition:** AUC = 0.5 means the model is **no better than random guessing** (diagonal line). AUC = 1.0 means **perfect separation**. AUC is also called the **discrimination ability** of the model. # 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why One Metric Is Never Enough In KTU exams and in industry practice, the choice of evaluation metric is **driven by the business problem**. The relationship between the four cells of the confusion matrix creates unavoidable trade-offs: - **Increasing Recall often decreases Precision** (catch more positives, but also catch more false ones). - **Increasing Precision often decreases Recall** (be more certain, but miss some positives). - **Accuracy can hide poor performance** on minority classes. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** *The Precision-Recall trade-off is governed by the classification threshold. As you lower the threshold, the model becomes more lenient in declaring "Positive," raising Recall but typically lowering Precision.* --- ## 2.2 The Supporting Metrics You Must Know Beyond the headline four, the KTU syllabus expects familiarity with two derived rates that appear on the ROC axes: **True Positive Rate (TPR) — same as Recall:** $$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ **False Positive Rate (FPR) — the Fall-out:** $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN}$$ **True Negative Rate (TNR) — Specificity:** $$TNR = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = 1 - FPR$$ **False Negative Rate (FNR) — Miss Rate:** $$FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = 1 - TPR$$ **Negative Predictive Value (NPV):** $$NPV = \frac{TN}{TN + FN}$$ **False Discovery Rate (FDR):** $$FDR = \frac{FP}{TP + FP} = 1 - \text{Precision}$$ --- ## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorization Mandate:** All formulas in the table below are examinable in both Part A (definitions) and Part B (numerical problems). | Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | Use Case | |--------|---------|-------|------------|----------| | **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Balanced datasets | | **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Cost of FP is high | | **Recall (TPR/Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Cost of FN is high | | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | True negative detection | | **FPR (Fall-out)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | [0, 1] | 0.0 | ROC x-axis | | **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2PR}{P+R}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Imbalanced data balance | | **Fβ-Score** | $\dfrac{(1+\beta^2)PR}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Weighted balance | | **AUC** | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | [0, 1] | 1.0 | Threshold-free evaluation | | **MCC (Matthews)** | $\dfrac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$ | [-1, 1] | 1.0 | Robust to imbalance | | **Cohen's Kappa** | $\dfrac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | [-1, 1] | 1.0 | Agreement beyond chance | --- ## 2.4 Why F-Measure Uses Harmonic Mean (Not Arithmetic) This is a classic KTU question. Suppose a model has Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.01. - **Arithmetic Mean:** $\frac{1.0 + 0.01}{2} = 0.505$ — misleadingly high. - **Harmonic Mean (F1):** $\frac{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.01}{1.0 + 0.01} = 0.0198$ — honest reflection of poor performance. > [!NOTE] > The **harmonic mean is always ≤ arithmetic mean**, and it heavily penalizes models that are extremely poor on either precision or recall. This is why F1 is preferred over a simple average for imbalanced classes. --- ## 2.5 ROC Curve Construction — Step-by-Step Theory 1. **Train your classifier** to output a probability score (e.g., logistic regression, SVM with `probability=True`). 2. **Sort all predictions** by descending predicted probability. 3. **Sweep the threshold** from 1.0 down to 0.0: - At threshold = 1.0: nothing is classified as Positive → TPR = 0, FPR = 0 (point at origin). - At threshold = 0.0: everything is classified as Positive → TPR = 1, FPR = 1 (point at top-right). 4. **At each threshold**, compute TPR and FPR and plot the point. 5. **Connect the points** to form the ROC curve. 6. **Reference Line:** The diagonal $y = x$ is the "random classifier" baseline. --- ## 2.6 AUC Interpretation Table (KTU Examiner's Favourite) | AUC Value | Interpretation | Grade | |-----------|----------------|-------| | 1.0 | **Perfect classifier** — perfect separation | A+ | | 0.9 – 0.99 | **Excellent** discrimination | A | | 0.8 – 0.89 | **Good** discrimination | B | | 0.7 – 0.79 | **Fair** discrimination | C | | 0.5 – 0.69 | **Poor** — close to random | D | | 0.5 | **No discrimination** — random guessing | F | | < 0.5 | **Worse than random** — labels inverted | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Probabilistic Interpretation of AUC:** *AUC equals the probability that a randomly selected Positive instance will be scored higher by the classifier than a randomly selected Negative instance.* --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | Preferred Metric | Why | |--------|------------------|-----| | **Medical Diagnosis** | Recall / F2 | Missing a disease (FN) is fatal | | **Spam Detection** | Precision | False alarms (FP) annoy users | | **Credit Card Fraud** | Recall + AUC | Missed fraud = direct money loss | | **Search Engines** | Precision@K | Users care about top-K relevance | | **Autonomous Driving** | Recall (object detection) | Missing a pedestrian is catastrophic | | **Quality Control** | Precision | Don't scrap good products | # 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Master Worked Example — Single Confusion Matrix, All Metrics ### 🧪 Problem Statement A binary classifier is evaluated on a held-out test set of **200 samples**. The results yield the following counts: $$TP = 70, \quad FP = 30, \quad TN = 80, \quad FN = 20$$ Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, F1-Score, FPR, FNR, NPV, FDR**, and **MCC**. --- ### 🔢 Step 1 — Verify the Total $$N = TP + FP + TN + FN = 70 + 30 + 80 + 20 = 200 \quad ✅$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 2 — Compute Accuracy Accuracy is the fraction of all correct predictions. $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{70 + 80}{200} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$ $$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 75.00\%}$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 3 — Compute Precision (PPV) Precision is the fraction of correct Positive predictions among all Positive predictions. $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70$$ $$\boxed{\text{Precision} = 70.00\%}$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 4 — Compute Recall (TPR / Sensitivity) Recall is the fraction of actual Positives that the model caught. $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{70}{70 + 20} = \frac{70}{90} \approx 0.7778$$ $$\boxed{\text{Recall} \approx 77.78\%}$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 5 — Compute Specificity (TNR) Specificity is the fraction of actual Negatives correctly identified. $$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273$$ $$\boxed{\text{Specificity} \approx 72.73\%}$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 6 — Compute the F1-Score We use the harmonic mean formula. $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$ $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.70 \cdot 0.7778}{0.70 + 0.7778}$$ $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5444}{1.4778}$$ $$F_1 = 2 \cdot 0.3684 = 0.7368$$ $$\boxed{F_1 \approx 0.7368 \;\; (73.68\%)}$$ --- ### 🔢 Step 7 — Compute FPR (Fall-out) and FNR (Miss Rate) $$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{30}{30 + 80} = \frac{30}{110} \approx 0.2727$$ $$FNR = \frac{FN}{FN + TP} = \frac{20}{20 + 70} = \frac{20}{90} \approx 0.2222$$ $$\boxed{FPR \approx 27.27\%, \quad FNR \approx 22.22\%}$$ **Sanity Check:** $FPR + Specificity = 0.2727 + 0.7273 = 1.0$ ✅ **Sanity Check:** $FNR + Recall = 0.2222 + 0.7778 = 1.0$ ✅ --- ### 🔢 Step 8 — Compute NPV and FDR $$NPV = \frac{TN}{TN + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$ $$FDR = \frac{FP}{FP + TP} = \frac{30}{30 + 70} = \frac{30}{100} = 0.30$$ $$\boxed{NPV = 80.00\%, \quad FDR = 30.00\%}$$ **Sanity Check:** $Precision + FDR = 0.70 + 0.30 = 1.0$ ✅ --- ### 🔢 Step 9 — Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) $$MCC = \frac{TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN}{\sqrt{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)(TN+FP)(TN+FN)}}$$ **Numerator:** $$TP \cdot TN - FP \cdot FN = (70)(80) - (30)(20) = 5600 - 600 = 5000$$ **Denominator (term-by-term):** $$TP + FP = 70 + 30 = 100$$ $$TP + FN = 70 + 20 = 90$$ $$TN + FP = 80 + 30 = 110$$ $$TN + FN = 80 + 20 = 100$$ **Product:** $$100 \cdot 90 \cdot 110 \cdot 100 = 99{,}000{,}000$$ **Square root:** $$\sqrt{99{,}000{,}000} \approx 9949.87$$ **Final MCC:** $$MCC = \frac{5000}{9949.87} \approx 0.5025$$ $$\boxed{MCC \approx 0.5025}$$ > [!NOTE] > MCC ranges from −1 to +1. A value of +0.50 indicates a **moderately strong positive correlation** between predictions and ground truth — significantly better than the +0.36 of a weak classifier. --- ## 3.2 ROC Curve Construction — Worked Numerical Example Suppose a classifier produces the following **ranked probability scores** for 10 instances (P = Positive actual, N = Negative actual): | Instance | Score | True Label | |----------|-------|------------| | 1 | 0.95 | P | | 2 | 0.90 | N | | 3 | 0.85 | P | | 4 | 0.70 | N | | 5 | 0.65 | P | | 6 | 0.60 | N | | 7 | 0.55 | P | | 8 | 0.40 | N | | 9 | 0.30 | P | | 10 | 0.20 | N | **Total Positives = 5, Total Negatives = 5** ### Step 1 — Build the ROC Points by Threshold Sweeping | Threshold | Predicted Positives | TP | FP | TPR | FPR | |-----------|---------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----| | ≥ 0.95 | {1} | 1 | 0 | 1/5 = 0.20 | 0/5 = 0.00 | | ≥ 0.90 | {1,2} | 1 | 1 | 0.20 | 0.20 | | ≥ 0.85 | {1,2,3} | 2 | 1 | 0.40 | 0.20 | | ≥ 0.70 | {1,2,3,4} | 2 | 2 | 0.40 | 0.40 | | ≥ 0.65 | {1,2,3,4,5} | 3 | 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 | | ≥ 0.60 | {1,2,3,4,5,6} | 3 | 3 | 0.60 | 0.60 | | ≥ 0.55 | {1..7} | 4 | 3 | 0.80 | 0.60 | | ≥ 0.40 | {1..8} | 4 | 4 | 0.80 | 0.80 | | ≥ 0.30 | {1..9} | 5 | 4 | 1.00 | 0.80 | | ≥ 0.20 | {1..10} | 5 | 5 | 1.00 | 1.00 | ### Step 2 — AUC via Trapezoidal Rule $$AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i) \cdot (TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2}$$ Applying the rule segment by segment: $$AUC = \frac{(0.20-0.00)(0.20+0.20)}{2} + \frac{(0.20-0.20)(0.40+0.20)}{2} + \cdots$$ Carrying out all 9 segments and summing: $$\boxed{AUC = 0.68}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** With **AUC = 0.68**, the model has a **68% probability** of correctly ranking a randomly chosen Positive higher than a randomly chosen Negative. This is a *fair* classifier. A perfect model on this data would achieve AUC = 1.0. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What | Situation | Recommended Metric | Reasoning | |-----------|--------------------|-----------| | Balanced classes, equal error costs | **Accuracy** | Simple, intuitive | | Imbalanced classes | **F1 / AUC / MCC** | Accuracy is misleading | | Missing positives is costly (medical) | **Recall / F2** | High sensitivity needed | | False alarms are costly (spam) | **Precision / F0.5** | Be conservative in declaring Positive | | Threshold-independent evaluation | **ROC-AUC** | Robust to threshold choice | | Multi-class problems | **Macro/Micro F1** | Average across classes | | Highly imbalanced with rare positives | **PR-AUC** | ROC is overly optimistic on imbalanced data | # 📕 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Confusion Matrix — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CM["Confusion Matrix - 2x2 Tabular Summary"] direction TB subgraph ROW1["ACTUAL CLASS ROW 1 = POSITIVE"] A1["TP: True Positive
Predicted P, Actually P
Correct Hit"] end subgraph ROW2["ACTUAL CLASS ROW 2 = NEGATIVE"] A2["FP: False Positive
Predicted P, Actually N
Type I Error"] A3["TN: True Negative
Predicted N, Actually N
Correct Reject"] A4["FN: False Negative
Predicted N, Actually P
Type II Error"] end end PRED1["PREDICTED POSITIVE COLUMN"] -.-> A1 PRED1 -.-> A2 PRED2["PREDICTED NEGATIVE COLUMN"] -.-> A3 PRED2 -.-> A4 A1 --> M1["Metric 1: Accuracy uses TP and TN"] A2 --> M2["Metric 2: Precision uses TP divided by TP plus FP"] A4 --> M3["Metric 3: Recall uses TP divided by TP plus FN"] A2 --> M4["Metric 4: FPR uses FP divided by FP plus TN"] ``` --- ## 4.2 ROC Curve Construction — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["STEP 1: Train Classifier
Output Probability Scores"] --> S2["STEP 2: Rank All Predictions
Sort by Score Descending"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Sweep Threshold
From 1.0 Down to 0.0"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: At Each Threshold
Compute TPR and FPR"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Plot TPR vs FPR
Connect the Dots"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: Compute Area
AUC Value"] S6 --> S7["DECISION GATE:
AUC Greater Than 0.5?"] S7 -- Yes --> S8["Model Has Skill
Better Than Random"] S7 -- No --> S9["Worse Than Random
Invert Predictions"] S5 -.-> DIAG["Diagonal Reference
Random Classifier Line"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Metric Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB Q1{"Is the dataset
balanced?"} -- Yes --> Q2{"Are error costs
equal?"} Q1 -- No --> Q3["Use F1-Score or AUC
Avoid Accuracy"] Q2 -- Yes --> Q4["Use Accuracy"] Q2 -- No --> Q5{"Which error is
more costly?"} Q5 -- "False Negative" --> Q6["Use Recall or F2
Medical, Fraud, Security"] Q5 -- "False Positive" --> Q7["Use Precision or F0.5
Spam, Quality Control"] Q3 --> Q8{"Need threshold
independence?"} Q8 -- Yes --> Q9["Use ROC-AUC"] Q8 -- No --> Q10["Use F1-Score"] ``` --- ## 4.4 Precision-Recall Trade-off Curve (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LOW["LOW THRESHOLD"] L1["Many Predictions Positive"] L2["Recall HIGH"] L3["Precision LOW"] end subgraph MID["MEDIUM THRESHOLD"] M1["Balanced Predictions"] M2["Recall MODERATE"] M3["Precision MODERATE"] end subgraph HIGH["HIGH THRESHOLD"] H1["Few Predictions Positive"] H2["Recall LOW"] H3["Precision HIGH"] end LOW ==>|"Raise Threshold"| MID MID ==>|"Raise Threshold"| HIGH ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Visual Insight:** As the threshold moves from LOW to HIGH, the model becomes **stricter** about declaring "Positive." Recall decreases monotonically while Precision increases monotonically — this is the foundational trade-off. # 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Precision and Recall. Explain why they are often in conflict with each other.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks) **Precision** is the ratio of correctly predicted Positive observations to the total predicted Positives. It answers: *"Of all instances labeled as Positive, how many were actually Positive?"* $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$ **Recall** (also called Sensitivity or True Positive Rate) is the ratio of correctly predicted Positive observations to all actual Positives. It answers: *"Of all actual Positive instances, how many did the model identify?"* $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$ **Why they conflict:** Precision and Recall are **inversely related** through the classification threshold. Lowering the threshold makes the model more lenient, catching more true positives (↑ Recall) but also admitting more false positives (↓ Precision). Raising the threshold makes the model stricter, yielding confident positives (↑ Precision) but missing some real positives (↓ Recall). Therefore, improving one often degrades the other, and the F1-score is used to find an optimal balance. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the ROC curve? Explain the significance of the Area Under the Curve (AUC).** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks) The **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve** is a 2D plot of the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis versus the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis, generated by varying the classification threshold of a binary classifier from 1.0 down to 0.0. The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a scalar summary of the entire ROC curve, ranging from 0.0 to 1.0. **Significance of AUC:** - **Threshold-independent** measure of classifier discrimination. - **AUC = 1.0:** Perfect classification. - **AUC = 0.5:** No better than random guessing. - **AUC < 0.5:** Worse than random (predictions inverted). - Probabilistically, AUC represents the **probability that the classifier ranks a random Positive instance higher than a random Negative instance**. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern ### ✏️ **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** A spam detection model produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of 500 emails: TP = 120, FP = 30, TN = 300, FN = 50. Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, Specificity, and F1-Score**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **limitations of accuracy** as an evaluation metric. Under what circumstances is F1-score preferred over accuracy? Illustrate with a numerical example. #### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Given:** TP = 120, FP = 30, TN = 300, FN = 50, Total N = 500. **Step 1 — Accuracy:** $$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{120 + 300}{500} = \frac{420}{500} = 0.84 \quad \text{(84.00\%)}$$ **Step 2 — Precision:** $$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 \quad \text{(80.00\%)}$$ **Step 3 — Recall (Sensitivity):** $$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 50} = \frac{120}{170} \approx 0.7059 \quad \text{(70.59\%)}$$ **Step 4 — Specificity (TNR):** $$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{300}{300 + 30} = \frac{300}{330} \approx 0.9091 \quad \text{(90.91\%)}$$ **Step 5 — F1-Score:** $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \cdot 0.7059}{0.80 + 0.7059}$$ $$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.5647}{1.5059} = 2 \cdot 0.3750 \approx 0.7500 \quad \text{(75.00\%)}$$ **Final Tabulated Answer:** | Metric | Value | |--------|-------| | Accuracy | 84.00% | | Precision | 80.00% | | Recall | 70.59% | | Specificity | 90.91% | | F1-Score | 75.00% | #### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Limitations of Accuracy:** 1. **Misleading on imbalanced data:** Consider a fraud detection dataset with 990 legitimate transactions and only 10 fraudulent ones. A naive model that always predicts "legitimate" achieves $\frac{990}{1000} = 99\%$ accuracy, yet it is **completely useless** because it detects zero frauds. 2. **Ignores error type costs:** Accuracy treats FP and FN as equally costly. In medical diagnosis, missing a cancer (FN) is far worse than a false alarm (FP). Accuracy cannot capture this asymmetry. 3. **No insight into class-wise performance:** A 90% accuracy could mean excellent performance on the majority class and terrible performance on the minority class — accuracy hides this. **When F1-Score Is Preferred:** - When the dataset is **imbalanced**. - When we need to **balance Precision and Recall** in a single number. - When the cost of FP and FN is **comparable but both matter**. **Numerical Illustration:** Consider a cancer detection model on 1000 patients where only 10 have cancer. | Scenario | TN | FP | FN | TP | Accuracy | F1 | |----------|----|----|----|----|----------|----| | Model A (predicts all "no cancer") | 990 | 0 | 10 | 0 | 99.0% | 0.00 | | Model B (catches some cancers) | 985 | 5 | 3 | 7 | 99.2% | 0.64 | Model A has 99% accuracy but F1 = 0 (useless). Model B has 99.2% accuracy AND F1 = 0.64 (genuinely useful). **F1 exposes the truth.** --- ### ✏️ **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ROC curve and AUC** in detail. How is the ROC curve constructed? What does an AUC of 0.85 indicate? **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Precision-Recall trade-off**. Compute and compare the F1 and F2 scores for a model with Precision = 0.75 and Recall = 0.60. Justify which one is more appropriate for **cancer detection**. #### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **ROC Curve:** A 2D plot with **TPR (Recall)** on the y-axis and **FPR = FP/(FP+TN)** on the x-axis. **Construction Steps:** 1. Train a probabilistic binary classifier. 2. Obtain probability scores for all test instances. 3. Sort predictions in descending order of score. 4. Sweep the threshold from 1.0 → 0.0. 5. At each threshold, compute (FPR, TPR). 6. Plot and connect all points. 7. The diagonal $y = x$ is the random-classifier baseline. **Key Properties of ROC:** - **Threshold-independent** evaluation. - **Insensitive to class prior** (class imbalance does not change the curve shape, only the threshold interpretation). - A model is **better** if its curve bulges **toward the top-left corner** (high TPR, low FPR). **AUC = 0.85 Interpretation:** - The model has an **85% probability** of ranking a randomly chosen Positive instance higher than a randomly chosen Negative instance. - Per the AUC interpretation table, this falls in the **"Good" (B-grade)** discrimination range. - The classifier is **substantially better than random** (which would be 0.5) but not yet "excellent" (≥ 0.90). #### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** Precision and Recall are governed by the **classification threshold**: - **Lower the threshold** → model predicts more Positives → Recall ↑, Precision ↓. - **Raise the threshold** → model predicts fewer Positives → Precision ↑, Recall ↓. This inverse relationship is fundamental. There is no threshold that maximizes both simultaneously — only a **Pareto-optimal frontier** exists. **F1-Score Computation (β = 1):** $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} = \frac{0.90}{1.35} \approx 0.6667$$ **F2-Score Computation (β = 2, Recall-weighted):** $$F_2 = \frac{(1 + 2^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{2^2 \cdot P + R} = \frac{5 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{4 \cdot 0.75 + 0.60} = \frac{2.25}{3.60} = 0.6250$$ **Comparison:** | Metric | Value | |--------|-------| | F1 | 0.6667 | | F2 | 0.6250 | **Justification for Cancer Detection:** In cancer screening, a **False Negative** (missing a cancer patient) is **catastrophic** — the patient loses a critical treatment window. A **False Positive** (false alarm) is undesirable but recoverable through follow-up tests. Therefore, **Recall must be weighted more heavily** than Precision. The **F2-score is more appropriate** than F1 because it penalizes false negatives (low recall) twice as much as false positives (low precision). Choosing F2 ensures the model prioritizes catching every possible cancer case, even at the cost of more follow-up screenings. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing the denominator of Precision vs Recall.** Many students write Precision = TP/(TP+FN) and Recall = TP/(TP+FP) — this is **reversed** and loses full marks. Memorize: *Precision cares about predicted Positives → denominator has FP. Recall cares about actual Positives → denominator has FN.* > > 2. **Using Accuracy on imbalanced data and calling the model "good."** Examiners deduct marks if you don't comment on the class distribution. Always pair Accuracy with a note on balance. > > 3. **Forgetting to subtract the diagonal in AUC trapezoidal computation.** A common numerical error is summing the entire rectangle area instead of using the trapezoidal rule. > > 4. **Misinterpreting AUC = 0.5 as "bad model"** — it means **no better than random**, not "broken." Examiners look for this nuance. > > 5. **Failing to justify the choice of metric in Part B.** You cannot simply compute F1 and stop. You must **justify** why F1 is preferred over Accuracy for the given problem (e.g., imbalanced data, cost asymmetry). > > 6. **Writing F-Measure = (P+R)/2** — this is the **arithmetic mean**, not the harmonic mean. The harmonic mean formula $\frac{2PR}{P+R}$ is **mandatory**. --- ## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2** - ✅ **Confusion Matrix** is the **foundation** of all classification metrics — always draw it first. - ✅ **Accuracy = (TP+TN)/N** — simple, but **misleading on imbalanced data**. - ✅ **Precision = TP/(TP+FP)** — *"Of those I called Positive, how many were right?"* - ✅ **Recall = TP/(TP+FN) = TPR = Sensitivity** — *"Of all the actual Positives, how many did I catch?"* - ✅ **Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = TNR = 1 − FPR**. - ✅ **F1-Score = 2PR/(P+R)** — **harmonic mean** (NOT arithmetic) of Precision and Recall. - ✅ **Fβ-Score** generalizes F1 with weight β on Recall: β > 1 prioritizes Recall, β < 1 prioritizes Precision. - ✅ **ROC Curve** plots TPR (y-axis) vs FPR (x-axis) across **all thresholds**. - ✅ **AUC** = probability of correct ranking; AUC = 1.0 is perfect, AUC = 0.5 is random. - ✅ **Precision-Recall Trade-off** is governed by the threshold — they move in **opposite directions**. - ✅ **MCC** and **Cohen's Kappa** are robust alternatives for **imbalanced datasets**. - ✅ **Choose the metric based on the cost of FN vs FP** — never default to accuracy in real problems. - ✅ Always **verify denominators** — Precision denominator has FP; Recall denominator has FN. - ✅ The **diagonal ROC line** ($y = x$) represents random guessing; curves above it are useful, below it are inverted. - ✅ **Trapezoidal Rule** is the standard method for AUC computation in exam numericals. --- **End of Module 2 — Evaluation Measures for Classification**

topics.title: Evaluation Measures for Regression - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation Measures for Regression - Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), R Squared/Coefficient of Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Regression Evaluation

In **Supervised Machine Learning**, once a regression model has been trained on a dataset, it is imperative to quantify *how well* the model generalizes to unseen data. Unlike classification (where we measure accuracy, precision, recall), regression deals with **continuous numerical outputs**, so the evaluation metrics must measure the *magnitude of error* between predicted and actual values.

The three foundational evaluation metrics mandated by the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Module 2 are:

1. **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**
2. **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**
3. **R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate (Module 2):** Every student must be able to *derive*, *compute*, and *critically compare* MAE, RMSE, and R². Simply stating the formula is **not sufficient** for full marks — examiners expect derivations, worked numerical examples, and practical interpretation.

---

## 1.2 Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — Formal Definition

**Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** is the average of the absolute differences between the predicted values ($\hat{y}_i$) and the actual observed values ($y_i$). It provides a straightforward, distance-based measure of prediction quality, expressed in the **same unit** as the original target variable.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

Where:
- $n$ = total number of data points
- $y_i$ = actual (true) value of the $i^{th}$ observation
- $\hat{y}_i$ = predicted value of the $i^{th}$ observation
- $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ = absolute residual (error) for the $i^{th}$ observation

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: The Arrows and the Target

Imagine you are an **archer** shooting arrows at a bullseye target.

- Each **arrow** is a model prediction ($\hat{y}_i$).
- The **center of the bullseye** is the true value ($y_i$).
- The **distance from the arrow to the center** is the error $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$.

**MAE is the average of all arrow-to-center distances.** A smaller MAE means your arrows are, on average, tightly clustered around the bullseye — a sign of an accurate model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property of MAE:** It treats *all errors equally* (linearly). A prediction that is off by 10 units is penalized exactly **twice** as much as one that is off by 5 units. There is **no squaring**, so **outliers do not dominate** the metric.

---

## 1.3 Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — Formal Definition

**Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** is the square root of the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values. It represents the **standard deviation of the residuals** (prediction errors) and tells us, on average, how far the predictions deviate from the true values.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Square Root?** We first square the errors to (a) eliminate negative signs, and (b) **amplify large errors disproportionately** (penalize outliers). Then we take the square root to bring the metric back to the **original unit of the target variable**, making it interpretable.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: The Arrows and the Target (Again)

Using the same archer analogy: **RMSE penalizes arrows that are far from the bullseye much more harshly than those that are close.** If one arrow wildly misses the target (a large outlier error), RMSE "punishes" the archer far more severely than MAE would. RMSE is the **strict, unforgiving coach**; MAE is the **gentle, fair coach**.

---

## 1.4 R-Squared (Coefficient of Determination) — Formal Definition

**R-Squared ($R^2$)**, also called the **Coefficient of Determination**, is a *normalized*, *unit-less* statistical measure that represents the **proportion of variance in the dependent variable ($y$) that is explained by the independent variables ($X$)** through the regression model.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

Where:
- $SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ = **Residual Sum of Squares** (unexplained variance)
- $SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$ = **Total Sum of Squares** (total variance in $y$)
- $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$ = mean of actual values

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range of R²:**
> - $R^2 = 1$: The model explains **100%** of the variance (perfect fit).
> - $R^2 = 0$: The model is **no better than simply predicting the mean** ($\bar{y}$) for every observation.
> - $R^2 < 0$: The model is **worse than the mean baseline** (a sign of a catastrophically poor model).
> - **Higher R² is better** (closer to 1).

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy: Explaining Exam Scores

Suppose a teacher wants to explain why some students scored higher than others on an exam. The **total variation** in scores is like the *total chaos* in the classroom. **R² tells us how much of that chaos the teacher can "explain" using factors like study hours, attendance, and prior GPA.**

- If $R^2 = 0.85$, the teacher can explain **85%** of why students differ in scores — only 15% remains a mystery (random noise).
- If $R^2 = 0.20$, only 20% of the variation is explained — 80% is unexplained.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Unlike MAE and RMSE, R² is a **relative measure** (between 0 and 1 for sensible models), making it **scale-independent**. It is used to compare models built on different datasets or with different units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of MAE — Step-by-Step

The MAE calculation follows a strict pipeline:

1. **Compute Residuals:** For each data point $i$, find the raw error:
   $$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$
2. **Absolute Value Transformation:** Take the magnitude to remove sign:
   $$\vert e_i \vert = \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$
3. **Summation:** Sum all absolute residuals:
   $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$
4. **Averaging:** Divide by $n$ to obtain the mean:
   $$MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

**Why does this work?**
- The absolute value $\vert \cdot \vert$ ensures that **positive and negative errors do not cancel out** (otherwise the sum could be artificially zero).
- Dividing by $n$ normalizes the metric to be independent of dataset size, allowing fair comparison across models.

**Engineering Utility of MAE:**
- Used in **demand forecasting** (retail, energy) where interpretability in original units is critical.
- Preferred when **outliers represent real anomalies** (e.g., fraud detection, medical diagnostics) and should not disproportionately skew the error metric.
- Common in **operations research** for inventory optimization.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of RMSE — Step-by-Step

The RMSE calculation has a similar but stricter pipeline:

1. **Compute Residuals:** $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$
2. **Square Each Residual:** $e_i^2 = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
   - This **amplifies large errors** quadratically.
   - A residual of 10 contributes **100**; a residual of 5 contributes only **25**.
3. **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE):** $SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
4. **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
5. **Square Root:** $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Square Root at the End?** MSE is in *squared units* (e.g., if $y$ is in meters, MSE is in $m^2$). Taking the square root returns the metric to the **original unit of measurement**, restoring physical interpretability.

**Engineering Utility of RMSE:**
- **Atmospheric and climate modeling** (temperature prediction in °C).
- **Financial risk modeling** (volatility forecasting in currency units).
- **Structural engineering** (deflection prediction in mm).
- Preferred when **large errors are particularly undesirable** and must be heavily penalized (e.g., safety-critical systems).

---

## 2.3 Operational Logic of R² — Step-by-Step

The R² metric requires understanding the decomposition of total variance:

1. **Compute the Mean of Actual Values:** $\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$
2. **Total Sum of Squares (SS_tot):** Measures the **total variance** in $y$ around its mean:
   $$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$
3. **Residual Sum of Squares (SS_res):** Measures the **unexplained variance** (model errors):
   $$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$
4. **Ratio of Unexplained to Total Variance:** $\frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$
5. **Subtract from 1:** $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$

**Intuitive Interpretation:**
- If $SS_{res} = 0$ (model predicts perfectly), then $R^2 = 1$.
- If $SS_{res} = SS_{tot}$ (model is no better than the mean), then $R^2 = 0$.
- The closer $R^2$ is to 1, the more variance the model "captures" or "explains."

**Engineering Utility of R²:**
- **Comparing regression models** on different scales (e.g., comparing a model predicting house prices in INR vs. predicting car mileage in km/l).
- **Model benchmarking** in scientific publications.
- **Explained variance analysis** in experimental physics and economics.

---

## 2.4 Critical Comparison: MAE vs RMSE vs R²

| **Property** | **MAE** | **RMSE** | **R²** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical Form** | $\frac{1}{n} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$ | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ |
| **Unit** | Same as target variable $y$ | Same as target variable $y$ | Dimensionless (unit-less) |
| **Range** | $[0, +\infty)$ | $[0, +\infty)$ | $(-\infty, 1]$ (typically $[0,1]$) |
| **Direction** | Lower is better | Lower is better | Higher is better |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | Low (linear penalty) | High (quadratic penalty) | Moderate |
| **Differentiability** | Not differentiable at 0 | Differentiable everywhere | Differentiable |
| **Best Use Case** | Outliers are real, robust eval | Penalize large errors, Gaussian noise | Compare models across datasets |
| **KTU 2024 Weightage** | High (derivation + numerical) | High (derivation + numerical) | High (interpretation + numerical) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation):** Students often write "RMSE is always better than MAE" or "R² > 0.9 means a good model." This is **incorrect**. Each metric has trade-offs. Examiners penalize absolute statements without contextual justification. Always mention the **data characteristics** (outliers, noise distribution, scale) when choosing a metric.

---

## 2.5 The Relationship: RMSE ≥ MAE (Mathematical Proof Sketch)

By the **Jensen's Inequality** applied to the convex function $f(x) = x^2$:

$$RMSE^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 \geq \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert \right)^2 = MAE^2$$

Therefore: $RMSE \geq MAE$, with equality **only when all residuals are equal in magnitude** (a perfectly uniform error distribution, which is rare in practice).

This inequality is **frequently asked in KTU exams** as a conceptual 3-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Raw Errors to MAE

**Starting Premise:** We have $n$ pairs of (actual, predicted) values: $(y_1, \hat{y}_1), (y_2, \hat{y}_2), \ldots, (y_n, \hat{y}_n)$.

**Step 1:** Define the residual for the $i^{th}$ observation:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$$

**Step 2:** Some residuals are positive (model under-predicts), some negative (model over-predicts). To prevent cancellation:

$$\vert e_i \vert = \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

**Step 3:** Sum across all $n$ observations:

$$\text{Total Absolute Error} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

**Step 4:** Normalize by $n$ to get the *mean*:

$$\boxed{MAE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert}$$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: From Raw Errors to RMSE

**Step 1:** Define the residual: $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$

**Step 2:** Square each residual to eliminate sign AND amplify large errors:

$$e_i^2 = (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 3:** Sum all squared residuals — this is the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$SSE = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the Mean Squared Error (MSE) by dividing by $n$:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 5:** Take the square root to return to the original unit of $y$:

$$\boxed{RMSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is MSE not used directly in KTU exams?** Because MSE is in *squared units* (e.g., $m^2$ instead of $m$), making it **non-interpretable** in practical terms. RMSE fixes this by taking the square root. KTU examiners prefer RMSE for final reporting.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Coefficient of Determination (R²)

**Step 1:** Compute the mean of actual values:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **Total Sum of Squares ($SS_{tot}$)** — this measures how much $y$ varies around its own mean (i.e., the total information in $y$):

$$SS_{tot} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **Residual Sum of Squares ($SS_{res}$)** — this measures the variation in $y$ that the model *failed* to explain:

$$SS_{res} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio of unexplained to total variance:

$$\text{Unexplained Ratio} = \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$$

**Step 5:** Subtract from 1 to get the **proportion of explained variance**:

$$\boxed{R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2}}$$

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** Consider a simple linear regression model trained on 4 data points. The actual and predicted values are given below. Compute **(i) MAE**, **(ii) MSE**, **(iii) RMSE**, and **(iv) R²**.

| **Index ($i$)** | **Actual ($y_i$)** | **Predicted ($\hat{y}_i$)** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.0 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 5.0 |
| 3 | 2.5 | 4.0 |
| 4 | 7.0 | 8.0 |

### **Step 1: Compute Residuals ($e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
e_1 &= 3.0 - 2.5 = 0.5 \\
e_2 &= 5.0 - 5.0 = 0.0 \\
e_3 &= 2.5 - 4.0 = -1.5 \\
e_4 &= 7.0 - 8.0 = -1.0
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 2: Compute the Mean of Actual Values ($\bar{y}$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\bar{y} &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = \frac{3.0 + 5.0 + 2.5 + 7.0}{4} = \frac{17.5}{4} = 4.375
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 3: Compute Absolute Residuals, Squared Residuals, and $(y_i - \bar{y})$**

| $i$ | $y_i$ | $\hat{y}_i$ | $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ | $\vert e_i \vert$ | $e_i^2$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3.0 | 2.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.25 | -1.375 | 1.890625 |
| 2 | 5.0 | 5.0 | 0.0 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 0.625 | 0.390625 |
| 3 | 2.5 | 4.0 | -1.5 | 1.5 | 2.25 | -1.875 | 3.515625 |
| 4 | 7.0 | 8.0 | -1.0 | 1.0 | 1.00 | 2.625 | 6.890625 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **3.0** | **3.50** | — | **12.6875** |

### **Step 4: Compute MAE**

$$\begin{aligned}
MAE &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert e_i \vert = \frac{1}{4} \times 3.0 = \mathbf{0.75}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the model's predictions deviate from the actual values by **0.75 units**.

### **Step 5: Compute MSE**

$$\begin{aligned}
MSE &= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \frac{1}{4} \times 3.50 = \mathbf{0.875}
\end{aligned}$$

### **Step 6: Compute RMSE**

$$\begin{aligned}
RMSE &= \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{0.875} \approx \mathbf{0.9354}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The typical prediction error magnitude is approximately **0.9354 units**. Notice $RMSE > MAE$ ($0.9354 > 0.75$), consistent with the inequality $RMSE \geq MAE$.

### **Step 7: Compute R² (Coefficient of Determination)**

$$\begin{aligned}
SS_{res} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = 0.25 + 0.00 + 2.25 + 1.00 = 3.50 \\
SS_{tot} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 1.890625 + 0.390625 + 3.515625 + 6.890625 = 12.6875 \\
R^2 &= 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{3.50}{12.6875} = 1 - 0.27584 \\
R^2 &\approx \mathbf{0.7242}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The model explains approximately **72.42%** of the variance in the target variable $y$. The remaining **27.58%** is unexplained variance (noise or features the model didn't capture).

---

## 3.5 Adjusted R² — The KTU Bonus Concept

A well-known **limitation of R²** is that it *always increases* (or stays the same) when you add more predictor variables to a regression model, even if those predictors are **irrelevant**. This is misleading.

**Adjusted R²** corrects for this by penalizing the addition of useless predictors:

$$\boxed{R^2_{adj} = 1 - \left( \frac{1 - R^2}{n - k - 1} \right) (n - 1)}$$

Where:
- $n$ = number of observations
- $k$ = number of predictor (independent) variables
- $R^2$ = standard coefficient of determination

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Adjusted R² is **only meaningful for multiple linear regression** (with multiple predictors). For **simple linear regression** (one predictor), Adjusted R² = R². Examiners often test this distinction.

**Key Properties of Adjusted R²:**
- It can **decrease** when an irrelevant predictor is added.
- It is always $\leq R^2$.
- It is the **preferred metric** for comparing multiple regression models with different numbers of features (e.g., in feature selection).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Regression Evaluation Pipeline — Functional Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid block-level architecture** maps the complete process of evaluating a regression model from raw predictions to final metric reporting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Trained Regression Model]) --> nodeB[Generate Predictions y_hat for all n samples]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Residuals: e_i = y_i minus y_hat_i]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Choose Evaluation Metric}
    nodeD -- MAE --> nodeE[Take absolute value of each residual]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Sum and divide by n to get MAE]
    nodeD -- RMSE --> nodeG[Square each residual]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Sum and divide by n to get MSE]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Take square root to get RMSE]
    nodeD -- R2 --> nodeJ[Compute mean of actual values y_bar]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute SS_res = sum of squared residuals]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Compute SS_tot = sum of squared deviations from mean]
    nodeK --> nodeM[R2 = 1 minus SS_res over SS_tot]
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeF --> nodeN([Report Final Metric])
    nodeI --> nodeN
    nodeM --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeO{Compare with Baseline and Other Models}
    nodeO -- Outliers Present --> nodeP[Prefer MAE for Robustness]
    nodeO -- Large Errors Critical --> nodeQ[Prefer RMSE for Strict Penalty]
    nodeO -- Cross Dataset Comparison --> nodeR[Prefer R2 for Scale Independence]
```

## 4.2 Comparative Decision Flowchart — Which Metric to Use?

This diagram helps KTU students **decide which evaluation metric is most appropriate** for a given regression problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Model Trained and Predictions Generated]) --> q1{Is Outlier Presence a Concern in the Dataset?}
    q1 -- YES, Outliers Exist --> q2{Is Interpretability in Original Units Critical?}
    q1 -- NO, Clean Data --> q3{Do You Need Cross Dataset Model Comparison?}
    q2 -- YES --> maefinal[Use MAE: Robust, Linear Penalty, Same Unit as Target]
    q2 -- NO --> rmse1[Use RMSE: Penalizes Large Errors Harshly]
    q3 -- YES --> r2final[Use R2: Scale Independent, Normalized 0 to 1]
    q3 -- NO --> q4{Are You Comparing Multiple Regression Models with Different Feature Counts?}
    q4 -- YES --> adjr2[Use Adjusted R2: Penalizes Useless Predictors]
    q4 -- NO --> rmse2[Use RMSE: Standard for Single Model Evaluation]
    maefinal --> done([End: Metric Selected])
    rmse1 --> done
    r2final --> done
    adjr2 --> done
    rmse2 --> done
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mapping of Error Decomposition (SS_tot = SS_reg + SS_res)

This diagram illustrates the **fundamental identity** that underlies R², which is a frequently tested concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sstot[SS_tot: Total Variance in y around its mean y_bar] --> decomposition{Orthogonal Variance Decomposition}
    decomposition --> ssreg[SS_reg: Explained Variance by the Model]
    decomposition --> ssres[SS_res: Unexplained Variance, Residual Error]
    ssreg --> r2formula[R2 = SS_reg divided by SS_tot]
    ssres --> r2formula2[R2 = 1 minus SS_res divided by SS_tot]
    r2formula --> output[If R2 close to 1: Model Captures Most Variance]
    r2formula2 --> output2[If R2 close to 0: Model Fails to Beat the Mean Baseline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Cue for Students:** The three-node variance decomposition is the *conceptual heart* of R². Examiners often award bonus marks if you can show that $SS_{tot} = SS_{reg} + SS_{res}$ using the projection geometry of least squares.
