topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees - Information Gain, Gain Ratio, ID3 Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Decision Trees — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both **classification (Decision Tree Classifier)** and **regression (Decision Tree Regressor)**. It models decisions and their possible consequences as a hierarchical, tree-like structure comprising a **Root Node**, **Internal Decision Nodes** (attribute tests), **Branches** (attribute values), and **Leaf/Terminal Nodes** (class labels or continuous output). The path from root to leaf represents a chain of **if-then-else classification rules**.

The **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm, proposed by **Ross Quinlan in 1986**, is the classical, top-down, greedy inductive learning algorithm that constructs a decision tree by recursively selecting the **best splitting attribute** at each node based on the metrics of **Information Gain (IG)** and **Gain Ratio (GR)** — both derived from **Claude Shannon's Entropy**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The 20-Questions Game**
> Imagine you are trying to guess whether a student will **Pass** or **Fail** an exam. Instead of asking random questions, you ask the *most informative* question first: *"Did the student attend more than 75% of classes?"* The answer **drastically reduces the uncertainty** (entropy). If the answer is "Yes," you follow up with another informative question like *"Did they complete all assignments?"*. This chain of smart questions forms a **Decision Tree**.
> - **Entropy** = How *mixed up* (uncertain) the group of students is.
> - **Information Gain** = How much *uncertainty is reduced* by asking a particular question.
> - **Gain Ratio** = A *fairness-adjusted* version of Information Gain that prevents the model from being biased toward questions that have too many possible answers.
> - **Leaf Node** = The final prediction (**Pass** or **Fail**).

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Entropy** | Measure of impurity / disorder in a dataset | $H(S)$ |
| **Information Gain** | Expected reduction in entropy due to splitting on attribute $A$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
| **Split Information** | Intrinsic value generated by splitting $S$ into $v$ partitions | $IV(S, A)$ |
| **Gain Ratio** | Normalized Information Gain, used by **C4.5** | $GR(S, A)$ |
| **Pure Node** | A node where all instances belong to a single class | — |
| **Root Node** | The topmost decision node (entire dataset) | $S$ |
| **Leaf Node** | Terminal node representing a class label | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Foundations — Building Blocks

The ID3 algorithm rests upon three primary mathematical quantities. Each one is derived from **Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**.

### 2.1.1 Shannon Entropy $H(S)$
Entropy quantifies the **average amount of information (in bits)** required to identify the class of a randomly drawn instance from set $S$. The higher the entropy, the **more impure/mixed** the dataset.

**Logical Breakdown:**
1. Compute the probability $p_i$ of each class $C_i$ in $S$.
2. Calculate $-\log_2(p_i)$, the **self-information** of $C_i$ (lower probability $\Rightarrow$ higher information).
3. Take the **expected value** of self-information across all classes.

### 2.1.2 Conditional Entropy $H(S, A)$
The expected entropy of $S$ *after* splitting on attribute $A$ (which has $v$ distinct values partitioning $S$ into subsets $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_v$).

### 2.1.3 Information Gain $IG(S, A)$
The **expected reduction in entropy** caused by partitioning $S$ according to attribute $A$. The attribute with the **highest IG** is chosen as the splitting criterion in classic ID3.

### 2.1.4 Split Information $IV(S, A)$ (Intrinsic Value)
The entropy of $S$ **with respect to the values of attribute $A$ itself** (not the class). It measures the **potential information generated by splitting** $S$ into $v$ partitions.

### 2.1.5 Gain Ratio $GR(S, A)$
A **normalized** version of Information Gain introduced by Quinlan in his successor algorithm **C4.5** to overcome the **multi-valued attribute bias** of ID3. If $|S_i| \approx |S|$ (very fine-grained split), $IV \to \log_2 v$ (large), so $GR$ is penalized.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you must memorize for the KTU End Semester Examination on this topic. The vertical bar delimiter `\vert` is used to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Formula | Range / Units | Remarks |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Entropy of Set $S$** | $H(S) = - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0,\; \log_2 c]$ **bits** | $p_i = \frac{\vert C_i \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ ; $0 \log 0 \equiv 0$ |
| 2 | **Binary Entropy** | $H(S) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p)\log_2(1-p)$ | $[0,\; 1]$ **bit** | Used when $c=2$ |
| 3 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_j)$ | bits | Weighted average of subset entropies |
| 4 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | bits | $IG \ge 0$ always |
| 5 | **Split Information (IV)** | $IV(S, A) = - \sum_{j=1}^{v} \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_j \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | bits | Same form as entropy, applied to $A$ |
| 6 | **Gain Ratio** | $GR(S, A) = \dfrac{IG(S, A)}{IV(S, A)}$ | dimensionless | Defined only when $IV(S, A) > 0$ |

---

## 2.3 The Multi-Valued Attribute Bias — Why Gain Ratio?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight — Real-world Utility of Gain Ratio**
> Information Gain has a critical flaw: it is **biased toward attributes that take many distinct values**. Example: consider an attribute `Student_ID` that uniquely identifies every student. Splitting on it produces $N$ pure subsets (each with entropy = 0), giving $IG \approx H(S)$, the **maximum possible**! Yet this attribute is *useless for prediction* on unseen data — a textbook case of **overfitting**.
> **Gain Ratio** divides by $IV(S, A)$, which is large for high-cardinality attributes. Hence, $GR$ "demotes" such attributes. C4.5 uses GR; ID3 uses raw IG. In KTU exams, you must always show **both** calculations when asked.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Use Case |
|:---|:---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Classifying tumours as malignant/benign |
| **Credit Scoring (Banking)** | Approve / reject loan applications |
| **Manufacturing Quality Control** | Predicting defective parts on assembly lines |
| **Telecom Churn Prediction** | Will a customer leave for a competitor? |
| **Intrusion Detection Systems (Cybersecurity)** | Classifying network traffic as benign / attack |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression classification |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate Active:** Every algebraic step, every arithmetic evaluation, and every log computation is shown explicitly. No "..." placeholders.

## 3.1 Master Worked Example — The "Play Tennis" Dataset

**Dataset (14 instances, 4 attributes, 2 classes: Yes / No):**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | **No** |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | **No** |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | **Yes** |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | **Yes** |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | **No** |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | **Yes** |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | **No** |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | **Yes** |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | **Yes** |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | **Yes** |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | **No** |

**Class Distribution:** 9 Yes, 5 No; Total $\vert S \vert = 14$.

---

### STEP 1 — Compute Entropy of the Full Dataset $H(S)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{2} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
     &= -\left[ p_{\text{Yes}} \log_2 p_{\text{Yes}} + p_{\text{No}} \log_2 p_{\text{No}} \right] \\
     &= -\left[ \frac{9}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) + \frac{5}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluating the logarithms:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) = \log_2(0.6429) = \frac{\ln(0.6429)}{\ln 2} = \frac{-0.4418}{0.6931} = -0.6374 \\
\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) = \log_2(0.3571) = \frac{\ln(0.3571)}{\ln 2} = \frac{-1.0296}{0.6931} = -1.4855
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\left[ (0.6429)(-0.6374) + (0.3571)(-1.4855) \right] \\
     &= -\left[ -0.4098 - 0.5305 \right] \\
     &= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\
     &= \mathbf{0.940 \; \text{bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### STEP 2 — Compute $H(S \mid \text{Outlook})$ and $IG(S, \text{Outlook})$

The attribute `Outlook` partitions $S$ into 3 subsets:

| Outlook Value | Count | Yes | No | Probability $p_{\text{Yes}}$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sunny | 5 | 2 | 3 | 0.40 |
| Overcast | 4 | 4 | 0 | 1.00 |
| Rain | 5 | 3 | 2 | 0.60 |

Computing subset entropies:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Sunny}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{5} + \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{5} \right] \\
                &= -\!\left[ (0.4)(-1.3219) + (0.6)(-0.7370) \right] \\
                &= 0.5288 + 0.4422 = \mathbf{0.9710}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Overcast}) &= -[ 1 \cdot \log_2 1 + 0 \cdot \log_2 0 ] = \mathbf{0.0000}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Rain}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{5} + \tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{5} \right] \\
               &= -\!\left[ (0.6)(-0.7370) + (0.4)(-1.3219) \right] \\
               &= 0.4422 + 0.5288 = \mathbf{0.9710}
\end{aligned}
$$

Weighted conditional entropy:
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0.0000) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) \\
                         &= 0.3571(0.9710) + 0 + 0.3571(0.9710) \\
                         &= 0.3468 + 0.3468 = \mathbf{0.6936}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IG(S, \text{Outlook}) &= H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) \\
                      &= 0.940 - 0.6936 = \mathbf{0.2464 \; \text{bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### STEP 3 — Compute $IG$ for All Remaining Attributes

**For `Temperature`** (Hot: 4, Mild: 6, Cool: 4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Hot}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4} + \tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{4}\right] = 1.000 \\
H(\text{Mild}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{4}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{6} + \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{6}\right] \\
               &= -(0.667)(-0.585) - (0.333)(-1.585) = 0.390 + 0.528 = 0.918 \\
H(\text{Cool}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{4} + \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{4}\right] = 0.811
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Temp}) &= \frac{4}{14}(1.000) + \frac{6}{14}(0.918) + \frac{4}{14}(0.811) \\
                     &= 0.2857 + 0.3934 + 0.2317 = 0.9108 \\
IG(S, \text{Temp}) &= 0.940 - 0.9108 = \mathbf{0.0292}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For `Humidity`** (High: 7 [3Y,4N], Normal: 7 [6Y,1N]):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{High}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{3}{7}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{7} + \tfrac{4}{7}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{7}\right] = 0.985 \\
H(\text{Normal}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{6}{7}\log_2\!\tfrac{6}{7} + \tfrac{1}{7}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{7}\right] = 0.592
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) &= \frac{7}{14}(0.985) + \frac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.4925 + 0.2960 = 0.7885 \\
IG(S, \text{Humidity}) &= 0.940 - 0.7885 = \mathbf{0.1515}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For `Wind`** (Weak: 8 [6Y,2N], Strong: 6 [3Y,3N]):

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Weak}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{6}{8}\log_2\!\tfrac{6}{8} + \tfrac{2}{8}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{8}\right] = 0.811 \\
H(\text{Strong}) &= -\!\left[\tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{6} + \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{6}\right] = 1.000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) &= \frac{8}{14}(0.811) + \frac{6}{14}(1.000) = 0.4634 + 0.4286 = 0.8920 \\
IG(S, \text{Wind}) &= 0.940 - 0.8920 = \mathbf{0.0480}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### STEP 4 — Root Node Selection & Gain Ratio Calculation

**Ranking by Information Gain:**

| Rank | Attribute | $IG$ (bits) | Split Info $IV$ | $GR = IG/IV$ |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | **Outlook** | **0.2464** | 1.577 | **0.1566** |
| 2 | Humidity | 0.1515 | 1.000 | 0.1515 |
| 3 | Wind | 0.0480 | 0.985 | 0.0487 |
| 4 | Temp | 0.0292 | 1.556 | 0.0188 |

**Detailed SplitInfo for `Outlook`** (showing full derivation):
$$
\begin{aligned}
IV(S, \text{Outlook}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{5}{14} + \tfrac{4}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{14} + \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{5}{14} \right] \\
                      &= -\!\left[ (0.3571)(-1.4855) + (0.2857)(-1.8074) + (0.3571)(-1.4855) \right] \\
                      &= 0.5305 + 0.5164 + 0.5305 = \mathbf{1.5774}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
GR(S, \text{Outlook}) = \frac{0.2464}{1.5774} = \mathbf{0.1562}
$$

> ✅ **Conclusion:** `Outlook` wins by **both** IG and GR → Selected as **Root Node**.

---

### STEP 5 — Recursion: Build Sub-Tree Under `Outlook = Sunny`

The `Sunny` subset has 5 instances (2 Yes, 3 No), so $H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = 0.971$.

| Attribute | Subsets (Yes/No) | $H(S_j)$ weighted | $IG$ |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Humidity** | High: 0/3, Normal: 2/0 | $0$ | **0.971** |
| Wind | Weak: 1/2, Strong: 1/1 | 0.751 | 0.020 |
| Temp | Hot: 0/2, Mild: 1/1, Cool: 1/0 | 0.400 | 0.571 |

`Humidity` is the best splitter for `Sunny`. After splitting:
- `Humidity = High` → All 3 instances are **No** (Leaf: **No**)
- `Humidity = Normal` → All 2 instances are **Yes** (Leaf: **Yes**)

Similar recursion on `Rain` branch picks `Wind` as the splitter, yielding pure leaves.

---

### STEP 6 — Final ID3 Decision Tree (Result of Recursion)

```
ROOT: Outlook
├── Overcast  →  LEAF: Yes
├── Sunny
│   └── Humidity
│       ├── High   →  LEAF: No
│       └── Normal →  LEAF: Yes
└── Rain
    └── Wind
        ├── Weak   →  LEAF: Yes
        └── Strong →  LEAF: No
```

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ID3 Algorithm — Top-Down Inductive Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Input Training Set S]) --> calcA["Step 1: Compute H of target class for current subset S"]
    calcA --> checkA{"Step 2: Is S pure? nbr All instances same class?"}
    checkA -- YES --> leafA["Create LEAF node nbr class label C"]
    checkA -- NO --> checkB{"Step 3: Are attributes exhausted?"}
    checkB -- YES --> leafB["Create LEAF node nbr majority class"]
    checkB -- NO --> loopA["Step 4: For EACH attribute A in remaining set:"]
    loopA --> loopB["Compute IG of S given A"]
    loopB --> loopC["If C4 dot 5: also compute GR = IG divide IV"]
    loopC --> loopD["Step 5: Select attribute A STAR with max IG or max GR"]
    loopD --> splitA["Step 6: Create DECISION node on A STAR"]
    splitA --> splitB["Partition S into S1 Sv by values of A STAR"]
    splitB --> rec1["Step 7: RECURSE on each Si with reduced attribute set"]
    rec1 --> stopA([END: Return constructed tree])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each `nbr` separator denotes a forced line break in the Mermaid label (rendered as a space in some viewers). All node IDs (`startA`, `calcA`, etc.) are alphanumeric to comply with the Mermaid safety protocol.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Information Flow During Tree Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DS[("DATASET S\nnbr nbr m instances nbr d attributes")] --> E["ENTROPY MODULE\nH S equals minus sum p log p"]
    E --> IGS["IG COMPUTATION\nIG S A equals H S minus H S given A"]
    DS --> CE["CONDITIONAL ENTROPY\nweighted sum of H Sj"]
    CE --> IGS
    IGS --> RANK{"RANKING\nSELECTOR"}
    CE --> IVC["INTRINSIC VALUE IV\nS A split info"]
    IVC --> GR["GAIN RATIO MODULE\nGR equals IG divide IV"]
    RANK -- max IG --> SELA["ID3 Decision:\nA STAR"]
    RANK -- max GR --> SELB["C4 dot 5 Decision:\nA STAR"]
    SELA --> SPLIT["PARTITION GENERATOR"]
    SELB --> SPLIT
    SPLIT --> REC["RECURSIVE CALL\nwith subset Si"]
    REC --> LEAF["LEAF NODE\nMajority class"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **same dataset feeds both IG and IV computation paths in parallel**. The `RANKING SELECTOR` is the configurable switch: it picks the best attribute using **IG for ID3** or **GR for C4.5**. The result drives the partition generator, which spawns recursive calls.

---

## 4.3 Gini vs Entropy — Comparative Schematic (Conceptual Reinforcement)

| Aspect | Entropy (ID3 / C4.5) | Gini Index (CART) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Formula basis | $H = -\sum p \log p$ | $G = 1 - \sum p^2$ |
| Computation cost | Slower (logarithm) | Faster (no log) |
| Range | $[0,\; \log_2 c]$ | $[0,\; 1 - 1/c]$ |
| Bias | Slightly favors balanced splits | Slightly favors larger partitions |
| Used by | ID3, C4.5, **C5.0** | **CART (Breiman, 1984)** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Information Gain. Why is it biased toward attributes with many values?
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3 | CO3 | Bloom: Understand]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Information Gain of an attribute $A$ with respect to a dataset $S$ is the **expected reduction in entropy** caused by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$:
$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

**Why biased? (2 Marks):** $IG$ is biased because attributes with **more distinct values** can split $S$ into many tiny, near-pure subsets. For example, an attribute like `ID` that uniquely identifies each instance yields a maximum $IG = H(S)$ because every subset becomes pure ($H=0$). Yet such an attribute is **useless for generalization** on unseen data — it causes overfitting. **Gain Ratio** normalizes $IG$ by the Split Information $IV(S, A)$ to counteract this bias, which is the central motivation for Quinlan's **C4.5** algorithm.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between ID3 and C4.5 algorithms.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3 | CO3 | Bloom: Analyze]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | ID3 | C4.5 |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Year / Author** | Quinlan, 1986 | Quinlan, 1993 |
| **Splitting Criterion** | Pure Information Gain | Gain Ratio (GR) |
| **Handles Continuous Attr.?** | No (categorical only) | Yes (uses threshold-based split) |
| **Handles Missing Values?** | No | Yes |
| **Pruning** | No inbuilt pruning | Includes error-based post-pruning |
| **Bias toward multi-valued** | Yes (high) | Corrected by GR |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> - **Common Pitfall 1:** Students compute $IG$ for *only* one attribute and directly state it as the answer, skipping the **entropy of the full set** and the **conditional entropies** of all subsets. **Deduct 2 marks** if not shown.
> - **Common Pitfall 2:** In Gain Ratio questions, forgetting to compute **Split Information $IV$** first. GR is undefined without it.
> - **Common Pitfall 3:** Using **$\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$** in entropy. KTU strictly expects **base-2** (units: bits). Using $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$ will lead to **incorrect units and wrong IG values**.
> - **Common Pitfall 4:** Stopping recursion when a node is *nearly pure* rather than strictly pure or attribute-exhausted. Decision tree growth is governed by **hard rules**, not heuristics.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Adaptation) | Module 3 | CO3 | Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following training data for a binary classification problem with attributes *Age*, *Income*, *Student*, *Credit Rating* and class *Buys_Computer*:

| Age | Income | Student | Credit Rating | Buys Computer |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Youth | High | No | Fair | No |
| Youth | High | No | Excellent | No |
| Middle | High | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Low | Yes | Excellent | No |
| Middle | Low | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Youth | Medium | No | Fair | No |
| Youth | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Youth | Medium | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | Medium | No | Excellent | Yes |
| Middle | High | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| Senior | Medium | No | Excellent | No |

**Compute the Information Gain for the attribute `Age` and select the best root-node attribute for the ID3 algorithm.** (You may also compute IG for the other three attributes for comparison.)

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to part (a), compute the **Gain Ratio** for all four attributes. Show that Gain Ratio overcomes the **multi-valued attribute bias** inherent in raw Information Gain. Draw the first two levels of the resulting ID3 decision tree.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

##### Part (a) — Computing $IG(S, \text{Age})$ [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Total Entropy $H(S)$**

Class distribution: 9 Yes, 5 No; $\vert S \vert = 14$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{9}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{9}{14} + \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{5}{14} \right] \\
     &= -\!\left[ (0.643)(-0.637) + (0.357)(-1.485) \right] \\
     &= 0.409 + 0.530 = \mathbf{0.940 \; \text{bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subset Entropies for `Age`**

- **Youth:** 5 instances (2 Yes, 3 No)
$$H(\text{Youth}) = -\!\left[\tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{5} + \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{5}\right] = 0.971$$

- **Middle-aged:** 4 instances (4 Yes, 0 No) → **Pure Node**
$$H(\text{Middle}) = 0$$

- **Senior:** 5 instances (3 Yes, 2 No)
$$H(\text{Senior}) = -\!\left[\tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{5} + \tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{5}\right] = 0.971$$

**Step 3 — Conditional Entropy $H(S \mid \text{Age})$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Age}) &= \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0) + \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) \\
                     &= 0.347 + 0 + 0.347 = \mathbf{0.694}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Information Gain $IG(S, \text{Age})$**
$$IG(S, \text{Age}) = 0.940 - 0.694 = \mathbf{0.246 \; \text{bits}}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with Other Attributes** (Results from Worked Example Section 3, Step 3):

| Attribute | $H(S \mid A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Age** | 0.694 | **0.246** ✅ |
| Income | 0.911 | 0.029 |
| Student | 0.789 | 0.152 |
| Credit Rating | 0.892 | 0.048 |

**Conclusion:** `Age` has the **highest Information Gain** → selected as the **Root Node** of the ID3 decision tree.

---

##### Part (b) — Gain Ratio Computation & Tree Construction [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Split Information $IV(S, A)$ for each attribute**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV(S, \text{Age}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{5}{14} + \tfrac{4}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{14} + \tfrac{5}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{5}{14} \right] \\
                  &= 0.531 + 0.516 + 0.531 = \mathbf{1.577}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV(S, \text{Income}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{4}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{14} + \tfrac{6}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{6}{14} + \tfrac{4}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{4}{14} \right] = 1.556
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV(S, \text{Student}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{7}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{7}{14} + \tfrac{7}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{7}{14} \right] = \mathbf{1.000}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV(S, \text{Credit}) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{8}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{8}{14} + \tfrac{6}{14}\log_2\!\tfrac{6}{14} \right] = 0.985
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Gain Ratio Table**

| Attribute | $IG(S, A)$ | $IV(S, A)$ | $GR(S, A) = IG/IV$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Age** | 0.246 | 1.577 | **0.156** ✅ |
| Income | 0.029 | 1.556 | 0.019 |
| Student | 0.152 | 1.000 | 0.152 |
| Credit Rating | 0.048 | 0.985 | 0.049 |

**Step 3 — Conclusion**

In this example, `Age` and `Student` are *very close* in GR (0.156 vs 0.152). **Note:** In general, GR changes the ordering relative to IG. The **multi-valued bias correction** is most visible when an attribute has extremely high cardinality (e.g., $IV$ becomes large, dragging $GR$ down). For example, if we had an attribute with 14 unique values (one per row), $IV \to \log_2 14 = 3.807$, which would suppress its $GR$ drastically.

**Step 4 — First Two Levels of the Decision Tree**

```
ROOT: Age
├── Youth   →  (Recurse: 2 Yes, 3 No)
├── Middle  →  LEAF: Yes  (pure node, 4 Yes, 0 No)
└── Senior  →  (Recurse: 3 Yes, 2 No)
```

Under `Youth` and `Senior` (each impure), continue recursion by recomputing IG over the *remaining* attributes (`Income`, `Student`, `Credit Rating`).

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper Adaptation) | Module 3 | CO3 | Bloom: Apply + Analyze]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** in detail. List its **steps** as a structured pseudocode-style sequence and state the **stopping criteria** clearly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the dataset below, compute the **Entropy of the target** and the **Information Gain** for the attribute `Outlook`. Also compute $IG$ for `Wind` and identify which attribute should be the root.

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

##### Part (a) — ID3 Algorithm Description [7 Marks]

**Definition (1 Mark):** ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3), introduced by **J. Ross Quinlan (1986)**, is a **top-down, greedy, recursive partitioning** algorithm that builds a decision tree by selecting the attribute that **maximizes Information Gain** at each node.

**Algorithm Steps (5 Marks):**

1. **Input:** A training set $S$ of instances, each described by $d$ categorical attributes $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_d$ and a class label $C$.
2. **Base Case Check:** If all instances in $S$ belong to the **same class** $C_k$, return a single leaf node labeled $C_k$.
3. **Base Case Check:** If the attribute set is **empty** (or all remaining attributes yield zero IG), return a leaf node labeled with the **majority class** in $S$.
4. **Selection:** For each candidate attribute $A_i$, compute the **Information Gain** $IG(S, A_i)$ using the entropy formula.
5. **Split:** Choose the attribute $A^*$ that yields the **highest** $IG(S, A_i)$. Make $A^*$ the decision attribute at the current node.
6. **Partition:** Partition $S$ into $v$ subsets $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_v$, where $S_j$ contains all instances with $A^* = a^*_j$.
7. **Recursion:** For each $j = 1, \dots, v$, apply the ID3 algorithm **recursively** on $S_j$ using the **reduced attribute set** $A \setminus \{A^*\}$.
8. **Output:** Return the constructed decision tree rooted at $A^*$.

**Stopping Criteria (1 Mark):**

- ✅ All instances in the current subset belong to a single class (pure node).
- ✅ No attributes remain for further splitting.
- ✅ The current subset is **empty** (create a leaf with parent's majority class).
- ✅ The maximum possible $IG$ for all remaining attributes is **zero** (no further information to extract).

---

##### Part (b) — Entropy and IG Computation [7 Marks]

**Class distribution:** 3 Yes, 3 No; $\vert S \vert = 6$.

**Step 1 — Total Entropy $H(S)$**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\!\left[ \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{6} + \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{6} \right] \\
     &= -\!\left[ 2 \cdot (0.5)(-1) \right] = \mathbf{1.000 \; \text{bit}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — $IG(S, \text{Outlook})$**

- **Sunny:** 2 instances, both No → $H = 0$
- **Overcast:** 1 instance, Yes → $H = 0$
- **Rain:** 3 instances, 2 Yes, 1 No
$$H(\text{Rain}) = -\!\left[\tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\!\tfrac{2}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{3}\right] = 0.528 + 0.528 = 0.918$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \tfrac{2}{6}(0) + \tfrac{1}{6}(0) + \tfrac{3}{6}(0.918) = 0.459 \\
IG(S, \text{Outlook}) &= 1.000 - 0.459 = \mathbf{0.541 \; \text{bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — $IG(S, \text{Wind})$**

- **Weak:** 4 instances, 3 Yes, 1 No
$$H(\text{Weak}) = -\!\left[\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{3}{4} + \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\!\tfrac{1}{4}\right] = 0.811$$

- **Strong:** 2 instances, 0 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) &= \tfrac{4}{6}(0.811) + \tfrac{2}{6}(0) = 0.541 \\
IG(S, \text{Wind}) &= 1.000 - 0.541 = \mathbf{0.459 \; \text{bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision**

| Attribute | $IG(S, A)$ |
|:---|:---:|
| **Outlook** | **0.541** ✅ |
| Wind | 0.459 |

**Conclusion:** `Outlook` is chosen as the **Root Node** for the ID3 decision tree.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things You Must Memorize Before Entering the KTU Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Entropy** is the *average self-information* of a probability distribution; **base-2 logarithm**; unit is **bits**; range is $[0, \log_2 c]$.
- ✅ $H = 0$ for a **pure node**; $H = 1$ for a **maximally impure binary split** (50-50).
- ✅ $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$; always **non-negative**.
- ✅ **Split Information** $IV(S, A)$ uses the same formula as entropy but applied to attribute $A$'s value distribution, not the class.
- ✅ **Gain Ratio** $= IG / IV$ — corrects the **multi-valued attribute bias** of $IG$.
- ✅ **ID3** uses IG; **C4.5** uses GR; **CART** uses **Gini Index** $G = 1 - \sum p_i^2$.
- ✅ Stopping criteria for tree growth: **purity**, **no attributes**, **empty subset**.
- ✅ ID3 is **greedy** (locally optimal at each node, not globally optimal).
- ✅ ID3 cannot natively handle **continuous attributes** or **missing values** — these are C4.5 improvements.
- ✅ Common confusion: **Entropy vs. Gini** — both measure impurity, but entropy uses $\log$ and Gini uses squared probabilities.
- ✅ **Always show $H(S)$ and $H(S \mid A)$ computations** in KTU answers — never skip to the final $IG$ value.
- ✅ $0 \log 0 \equiv 0$ by convention (L'Hôpital's limit of $x \log x \to 0$ as $x \to 0$).
- ✅ **Real-world applications:** medical diagnosis, credit scoring, churn prediction, fault detection, spam filtering, image classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Unsupervised Learning Clustering - Similarity Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Unsupervised Learning**, **Clustering** is the process of partitioning an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ into $k$ homogeneous groups (clusters) $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that intra-cluster similarity is **maximized** and inter-cluster similarity is **minimized**. The engine that quantifies this "homogeneity" is the **Similarity Measure** (or its dual, the **Dissimilarity/Distance Measure**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** A **Similarity Measure** $S(x_i, x_j)$ is a scalar function that quantifies the degree to which two feature vectors $x_i$ and $x_j$ are alike, while a **Distance (Dissimilarity) Measure** $d(x_i, x_j)$ quantifies how far apart they are. Formally, $S(x_i, x_j) = \frac{1}{1 + d(x_i, x_j)}$ for many standard metrics.

**Metric Axioms (Kant's Distance Properties):** Any valid distance function $d(x, y)$ on a vector space must satisfy the following four **mandatory axioms**:

1. **Non-negativity:** $d(x, y) \geq 0$
2. **Identity of Indiscernibles:** $d(x, y) = 0 \iff x = y$
3. **Symmetry:** $d(x, y) = d(y, x)$
4. **Triangle Inequality:** $d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z)$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** The **Cosine Similarity** is *not* a true metric because it violates the triangle inequality. Examiners frequently test this conceptual nuance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **Zoo Curator** tasked with grouping 50 newly arrived animals into enclosures *without being told their species*. You would naturally look at measurable attributes — height, weight, tail length, number of legs, daily food intake — and place "visually similar" creatures together.

- The **animal's attribute list** is the *feature vector* $x_i$.
- The **"visually similar" judgment** is computed mathematically via a *similarity measure*.
- The **enclosures** are the *clusters* $C_k$.
- The **head curator's rulebook** that decides who goes where is the *clustering algorithm* (e.g., K-Means, Hierarchical).

Two elephants will have a **small Euclidean distance** (high similarity) because their attribute vectors lie close in the 5-dimensional attribute space, while an elephant and a sparrow will be **far apart**. The Similarity Measure is the mathematical ruler we use to measure that "closeness."

### 1.3 Classification of Measures

Similarity measures are broadly classified into two families based on data type:

| Family | Data Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Distance-Based (Metric)** | Continuous / Numerical | Euclidean, Manhattan, Minkowski, Chebyshev, Mahalanobis |
| **Correlation / Angle-Based** | Sparse / Text / High-Dim | Cosine Similarity, Pearson Correlation |
| **Set-Based** | Binary / Categorical | Jaccard, Hamming, Tanimoto |

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb:** Use **Euclidean** for low-dimensional dense continuous data, **Manhattan** for high-dimensional sparse data, **Cosine** for text/document vectors, and **Mahalanobis** when features are correlated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

A clustering algorithm is essentially an **iterative optimization procedure** that relies on a similarity measure as its scoring function. The general pipeline is:

1. **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose initial cluster centroids $\mu_k$ (or initial partition).
2. **Step 2 — Assignment:** For every data point $x_i$, compute $d(x_i, \mu_k)$ for all $k$ clusters and assign $x_i$ to the cluster with the **minimum distance** (or maximum similarity).
3. **Step 3 — Update:** Recompute the centroids $\mu_k$ as the mean of all points assigned to that cluster.
4. **Step 4 — Convergence:** Repeat Steps 2–3 until assignments no longer change (i.e., $J = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{x_i \in C_k\} \cdot d(x_i, \mu_k)$ stabilizes).

The "How" depends entirely on the choice of $d(\cdot, \cdot)$. The "Why" is that a *well-chosen* similarity measure creates a feature-space geometry that aligns with the true underlying group structure of the data.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Let $x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n)$ be two $n$-dimensional feature vectors.

| # | Measure | Formula | Key Use Case | Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Euclidean Distance ($L_2$)** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Most common, K-Means default | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 2 | **Squared Euclidean** | $d^2(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - y_i)^2$ | Computational shortcut (no sqrt) | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 3 | **Manhattan / City-Block ($L_1$)** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | High-dim, grid-like paths | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 4 | **Minkowski ($L_p$)** | $d(x,y) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert x_i - y_i \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized family | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 5 | **Chebyshev ($L_\infty$)** | $d(x,y) = \max_{i} \vert x_i - y_i \vert$ | "Worst-case" deviation | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 6 | **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\vert\vert x \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert y \vert\vert}$ | Text mining, document clustering | $[-1, +1]$ |
| 7 | **Cosine Distance** | $d_c(x,y) = 1 - \cos(\theta)$ | Dual of Cosine Similarity | $[0, 2]$ |
| 8 | **Jaccard Coefficient** | $J(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Binary sets, basket analysis | $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | **Hamming Distance** | $d_H(x,y) = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}[x_i \neq y_i]$ | Binary/categorical strings | $[0, n]$ |
| 10 | **Mahalanobis Distance** | $d_M(x,y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-y)}$ | Correlated features (uses covariance $\Sigma$) | $[0, \infty)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - For $p=1$, Minkowski $\rightarrow$ Manhattan.
> - For $p=2$, Minkowski $\rightarrow$ Euclidean.
> - For $p \to \infty$, Minkowski $\rightarrow$ Chebyshev.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Euclidean** — Image segmentation, anomaly detection in IoT sensor streams, K-Means customer segmentation in retail analytics.
- **Cosine** — Search engines (TF-IDF document matching), recommender systems (collaborative filtering), plagiarism detection.
- **Mahalanobis** — Quality control in manufacturing (correlated process variables), bioinformatics (gene expression with correlated markers).
- **Jaccard** — Market-basket analysis, chemical fingerprint similarity in drug discovery, IoT device clustering by behavioral signatures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minkowski Family as a Unification of $L_p$ Norms

The Minkowski distance is derived from the **$L_p$ vector norm** definition. The $L_p$ norm of a vector $v = (v_1, \ldots, v_n)$ is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|v\|_p &= \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |v_i|^p \right)^{1/p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $v = x - y$, where $x - y = (x_1 - y_1, \ldots, x_n - y_n)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|x - y\|_p &= \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^p \right)^{1/p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** By definition, the distance equals this norm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_p(x, y) &= \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^p \right)^{1/p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (Specialization):** Set $p = 1$ to obtain the Manhattan distance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1(x, y) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (Specialization):** Set $p = 2$ to obtain the Euclidean distance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2(x, y) &= \sqrt{ \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2 }
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (Limit Case):** As $p \to \infty$, the term with the largest absolute difference dominates, yielding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_\infty(x, y) &= \lim_{p \to \infty} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^p \right)^{1/p} = \max_{i} |x_i - y_i|
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Chebyshev distance**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Frequently Asked Style)

**Problem:** Compute the Euclidean, Manhattan, Chebyshev, and Cosine distances between the two vectors:

$$
x = (1, 2, 3), \qquad y = (4, 6, 3)
$$

**Solution — Step 1: Compute element-wise differences**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta &= x - y = (1-4, \; 2-6, \; 3-3) = (-3, -4, 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 2: Euclidean Distance ($L_2$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_E(x, y) &= \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-4)^2 + (0)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{9 + 16 + 0} \\
&= \sqrt{25} = \mathbf{5.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 3: Manhattan Distance ($L_1$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_M(x, y) &= |-3| + |-4| + |0| = 3 + 4 + 0 = \mathbf{7.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 4: Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_C(x, y) &= \max(|-3|, |-4|, |0|) = \max(3, 4, 0) = \mathbf{4.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 5: Cosine Similarity**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x \cdot y &= (1)(4) + (2)(6) + (3)(3) = 4 + 12 + 9 = 25 \\
\|x\| &= \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4 + 9} = \sqrt{14} \\
\|y\| &= \sqrt{4^2 + 6^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{16 + 36 + 9} = \sqrt{61} \\
\cos(\theta) &= \frac{25}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{61}} = \frac{25}{\sqrt{854}} \approx \frac{25}{29.223} \approx \mathbf{0.8556}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution — Step 6: Cosine Distance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{\cos}(x, y) &= 1 - 0.8556 = \mathbf{0.1444}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The two vectors are *very close in direction* (cosine $\approx 0.86$) but *moderately far in magnitude* (Euclidean $= 5$). This is why cosine is preferred for *orientation* matching (text retrieval) while Euclidean is preferred for *positional* matching (K-Means).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Euclidean | Manhattan | Cosine | Mahalanobis | Jaccard |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Satisfies Triangle Inequality? | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ (as $1-J$) |
| Scale Invariant? | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ (covariance-scaled) | ✓ |
| Handles Correlation? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Works on Binary? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Computational Cost | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Sensitive to Outliers? | **High** | Medium | Low | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Similarity-Based Clustering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unlabeled Dataset X] --> B[Feature Extraction and Normalization]
    B --> C{Choose Similarity Measure}
    C --> D1[Euclidean L2]
    C --> D2[Manhattan L1]
    C --> D3[Cosine Similarity]
    C --> D4[Mahalanobis]
    C --> D5[Jaccard]
    D1 --> E[Pairwise Distance Matrix D]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Clustering Algorithm K Means or Hierarchical]
    F --> G[Cluster Assignment Labels]
    G --> H[Validation Silhouette Index or DB Index]
    H --> I{Meets Quality Threshold?}
    I -- No --> J[Tune Similarity Measure or Parameters]
    J --> C
    I -- Yes --> K[Final Clusters C1 C2 ... Ck]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree for Selecting the Right Similarity Measure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Data Type] --> Q1{Data Type?}
    Q1 -- Continuous Numerical --> Q2{Feature Dimension?}
    Q1 -- Text or Sparse --> M3[Use Cosine Similarity]
    Q1 -- Binary Set --> M4[Use Jaccard Coefficient]
    Q2 -- Low Dimensional --> M1[Use Euclidean Distance]
    Q2 -- High Dimensional --> Q3{Correlated Features?}
    Q3 -- No --> M2[Use Manhattan Distance]
    Q3 -- Yes --> M5[Use Mahalanobis Distance]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Visualization: Concentric Cluster Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Point A 1 2] --> CENTER[Centroid Mu 3 4]
    P2[Point B 2 3] --> CENTER
    P3[Point C 4 5] --> CENTER
    P4[Point D 8 9] -. Far Cluster 2 .-> CENTER2[Centroid Nu 9 9]
    P5[Point E 10 10] --> CENTER2
    P6[Point F 9 8] --> CENTER2
    CENTER ---|Small d E Intra Cluster| CENTER2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define a similarity measure. List the four metric axioms that any valid distance function must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A similarity measure is a scalar function $S(x, y)$ that quantifies the degree of resemblance between two feature vectors, while a distance (dissimilarity) measure $d(x, y)$ quantifies their separation. The two are related by monotonic transformations, e.g., $S = 1/(1+d)$.

The four **metric axioms** are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i) Non-negativity: } d(x, y) \geq 0 \\
&\text{(ii) Identity: } d(x, y) = 0 \iff x = y \\
&\text{(iii) Symmetry: } d(x, y) = d(y, x) \\
&\text{(iv) Triangle inequality: } d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z)
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the four axioms with brief descriptions.)*

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Euclidean distance and Cosine similarity. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Euclidean | Cosine |
|---|---|---|
| Measures | Magnitude difference | Angular (orientation) difference |
| Formula | $\sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $\frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\| \|y\|}$ |
| Range | $[0, \infty)$ | $[-1, 1]$ |
| Scale Sensitive | Yes | No |
| Use Case | K-Means, sensor data | Document clustering, TF-IDF |

**Preference Rule:** Use **Euclidean** when the *absolute magnitude* of the feature vector carries meaning (e.g., physical measurements like height, weight). Use **Cosine** when only the *direction/orientation* matters and vector magnitude is arbitrary (e.g., text documents where document length varies).

*(Award 1.5 marks for the difference, 1.5 marks for the use-case justification.)*

---

### PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat equations, explain the Minkowski distance metric. Show that it reduces to Euclidean, Manhattan, and Chebyshev distances for specific values of the parameter $p$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the data points $P_1 = (2, 3, 4)$, $P_2 = (5, 8, 1)$, and $P_3 = (0, 2, 1)$, construct the complete $3 \times 3$ pairwise Euclidean distance matrix and identify which two points are the most similar.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Minkowski Distance Explanation**

The Minkowski distance of order $p$ between two $n$-dimensional vectors $x$ and $y$ is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_p(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^p \right)^{1/p}, \quad p \geq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **generalized family** parameterized by the scalar $p$, which controls the order of the norm.

**Specialization 1: $p = 1$ → Manhattan Distance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_1(x, y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|
\end{aligned}
$$

**Specialization 2: $p = 2$ → Euclidean Distance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_2(x, y) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^2 \right)^{1/2} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Specialization 3: $p \to \infty$ → Chebyshev Distance**

As $p$ grows, the term with the largest $|x_i - y_i|$ dominates, and the limit yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_\infty(x, y) = \lim_{p \to \infty} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} |x_i - y_i|^p \right)^{1/p} = \max_{i \in \{1,\ldots,n\}} |x_i - y_i|
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Significance:** The Minkowski family unifies all $L_p$ norms, providing a tunable trade-off between sensitivity to small vs. large coordinate differences. **(1 mark for each correct specialization, 1 mark for the significance, totaling 7.)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Pairwise Euclidean Distance Matrix**

**Step 1: Compute $d(P_1, P_2)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_2) &= \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (3-8)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 25 + 9} = \sqrt{43} \approx 6.557
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $d(P_1, P_3)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_1, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2-0)^2 + (3-2)^2 + (4-1)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 1 + 9} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $d(P_2, P_3)$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(P_2, P_3) &= \sqrt{(5-0)^2 + (8-2)^2 + (1-1)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 36 + 0} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.810
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Construct the Symmetric Matrix** (diagonal = 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 6.557 & 3.742 \\ 6.557 & 0 & 7.810 \\ 3.742 & 7.810 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Identify the Most Similar Pair**

The smallest off-diagonal entry is $d(P_1, P_3) \approx 3.742$.

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{P_1 \text{ and } P_3 \text{ are the most similar pair.}}$

> *(Award 1.5 marks per distance computation, 1 mark for matrix construction, 0.5 mark for identifying the minimum.)*

---

#### **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Cosine similarity and Cosine distance. Prove that Cosine similarity is bounded in $[-1, 1]$ using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider two documents represented as term-frequency vectors $D_1 = (3, 0, 2, 1)$ and $D_2 = (1, 0, 4, 0)$. Compute (i) Cosine similarity, (ii) Cosine distance, and (iii) interpret the result for a text-clustering application.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Cosine Similarity and Cauchy-Schwarz Proof**

**Definition:** The Cosine similarity between two non-zero vectors $x, y \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\theta) = S_C(x, y) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\|x\|_2 \cdot \|y\|_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The corresponding **Cosine distance** is $d_C(x, y) = 1 - \cos(\theta)$.

**Proof of Bound Using Cauchy-Schwarz:**

The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality states that for any vectors $u, v \in \mathbb{R}^n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|u \cdot v| \leq \|u\|_2 \cdot \|v\|_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $u = x$ and $v = y$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
|x \cdot y| &\leq \|x\|_2 \cdot \|y\|_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by the positive quantity $\|x\|_2 \cdot \|y\|_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{|x \cdot y|}{\|x\|_2 \cdot \|y\|_2} &\leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-1 \leq \cos(\theta) \leq +1
\end{aligned}
$$

The lower bound $\cos(\theta) = -1$ occurs when $x$ and $y$ are anti-parallel; $\cos(\theta) = +1$ occurs when they are parallel.

> *(Award 2 marks for the definition, 3 marks for the Cauchy-Schwarz application, 2 marks for stating the final bound and extreme cases.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] — Document Vector Computation**

Given $D_1 = (3, 0, 2, 1)$ and $D_2 = (1, 0, 4, 0)$.

**Step (i) — Cosine Similarity:**

Dot product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 \cdot D_2 &= (3)(1) + (0)(0) + (2)(4) + (1)(0) = 3 + 0 + 8 + 0 = 11
\end{aligned}
$$

Magnitudes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|D_1\| &= \sqrt{3^2 + 0^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{9 + 0 + 4 + 1} = \sqrt{14} \approx 3.742 \\
\|D_2\| &= \sqrt{1^2 + 0^2 + 4^2 + 0^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0 + 16 + 0} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123
\end{aligned}
$$

Cosine Similarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\cos(\theta) &= \frac{11}{\sqrt{14} \cdot \sqrt{17}} = \frac{11}{\sqrt{238}} \approx \frac{11}{15.427} \approx \mathbf{0.7130}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Cosine Distance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_C(D_1, D_2) &= 1 - 0.7130 = \mathbf{0.2870}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (iii) — Interpretation for Text Clustering:**

A cosine similarity of $\approx 0.71$ indicates that the two documents share a **strong topical overlap** (small angle between their term-frequency vectors in the 4-D term space). They discuss the same general subject matter but differ in specific keyword emphasis (note the differing weights on terms 1 and 3).

For text clustering, this pair would be placed in the **same cluster** if a threshold of, say, $\cos(\theta) > 0.5$ is used. The cosine measure correctly handles the fact that $D_2$ is shorter than $D_1$ (only 2 non-zero terms vs. 3), focusing on *topic direction* rather than *document length*.

> *(Award 2 marks for dot product and magnitudes, 1.5 marks for similarity, 1 mark for distance, 2.5 marks for the correct interpretation with thresholding logic.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Manhattan with Mahalanobis** — Manhattan is a simple coordinate-wise sum, while Mahalanobis *requires the inverse covariance matrix*. Examiners deduct 2 marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting the square root** in Euclidean or the $(1/p)$ exponent in Minkowski is a common 1-mark penalty.
> 3. **Cosine vs. Cosine Distance:** Students often write $1 - \cos\theta$ for *similarity* instead of *distance*. The KTU key requires students to state *which one* is being asked.
> 4. **Hamming distance** is *not* defined on continuous real-valued vectors — using it on $D_1, D_2$ above would be incorrect.
> 5. **Scale dependence:** Always perform **z-score normalization or min-max scaling** before applying Euclidean/Manhattan. Forgetting this is a frequent 2-mark deduction in KTU papers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Similarity Measure** quantifies likeness; **Distance Measure** quantifies separation. They are monotonic duals.
- ✅ The four **metric axioms** (non-negativity, identity, symmetry, triangle inequality) define a true metric.
- ✅ **Minkowski family** unifies Manhattan ($p=1$), Euclidean ($p=2$), and Chebyshev ($p \to \infty$).
- ✅ **Euclidean** is the default in K-Means; sensitive to outliers and scale.
- ✅ **Cosine similarity** measures *angular orientation*, not magnitude; bounded in $[-1, 1]$ by Cauchy-Schwarz.
- ✅ **Mahalanobis** is the only metric that accounts for feature correlation via $\Sigma^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Jaccard** is restricted to *binary* or *set* data; equal to 1 minus the Jaccard distance.
- ✅ **Hamming** counts mismatched positions in binary/categorical strings.
- ✅ Always **normalize** your data before applying distance metrics to prevent feature-scale bias.
- ✅ Memorize the **cheat-sheet table** in §2.2 — it is the single most-tested artifact in KTU Module 4.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Clustering - Agglomerative Clustering, Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hierarchical Clustering - Agglomerative Clustering, Partitional Clustering, K-Means Clustering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Clustering: The Unsupervised Learning Paradigm

**Clustering** is an *unsupervised learning* technique whose objective is to partition an unlabeled dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ into $k$ meaningful groups (clusters) such that intra-cluster similarity is **maximised** and inter-cluster similarity is **minimised**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Clustering is the process of organising data objects into groups whose members are similar in some way. A cluster is therefore a collection of data objects that are similar to one another within the same cluster and dissimilar to objects in other clusters.*

---

## 1.2 Hierarchical Clustering — Formal Definition

**Hierarchical Clustering** builds a multi-level hierarchy of clusters by either repeatedly *merging* smaller clusters into larger ones (agglomerative) or *splitting* a large cluster into smaller ones (divisive). The result is typically visualised as a tree-like structure called a **dendrogram**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Hierarchical clustering does **not** require the number of clusters $k$ to be specified a priori — it produces a *full hierarchy* from which any number of clusters can be extracted by cutting the dendrogram at a chosen height.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Tree of Data"

Imagine you have 20 strangers at a party and want to group them by personality. You start by introducing the two most similar people and pairing them. Then you bring in the next most compatible person or pair, and keep grouping. Eventually you have a **family tree** showing how everyone was merged from individual people into ever-larger social groups. That family tree is exactly what a dendrogram represents for data.

---

## 1.3 Agglomerative Clustering (Bottom-Up)

**Agglomerative Clustering** is a *bottom-up* hierarchical method. The procedure is:

1. Start with $n$ singleton clusters (each data point is its own cluster).
2. Repeatedly merge the **two closest clusters** based on a chosen **linkage criterion**.
3. Stop when only one cluster (the root) remains, or when a stopping criterion is met.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Term:** *AGNES (Agglomerative Nesting)* is the classical name for this algorithm.

---

## 1.4 Partitional Clustering — Formal Definition

**Partitional Clustering** divides the dataset into a *single-level*, non-overlapping partition of $k$ clusters such that every data point belongs to **exactly one cluster**. Unlike hierarchical methods, it does **not** produce a nested tree — it gives one flat partition.

The objective is typically to minimise a global cost function such as the **sum of squared errors (SSE)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Grouping"

A teacher has 60 students and must divide them into 4 project groups. She looks at all 60 together, decides on 4 group captains (centroids), and assigns each student to the most suitable captain. The result is a flat, single-level grouping — not a family tree. This is partitional clustering.

---

## 1.5 K-Means Clustering — Formal Definition

**K-Means Clustering** is the most widely used *centroid-based* partitional clustering algorithm. It partitions $n$ data points into $k$ clusters by minimising the **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *K-Means is an iterative partitional clustering algorithm that assigns each data point to the cluster with the nearest centroid, where the centroid is the mean of all points belonging to that cluster. The algorithm alternates between assignment and centroid update steps until convergence.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cell Tower Coverage"

Imagine a city where you must place exactly $k = 4$ cell towers such that every household connects to the *closest* tower. You place the towers somewhere, then connect each household to the nearest one. Once connected, you move each tower to the *centre* of all its connected households. You repeat this "assign → reposition" loop until no household wants to switch towers. The final positions of the 4 towers are the K-Means centroids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric:** The optimisation objective in K-Means is the **inertia** or **WCSS**, formally defined in Section 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Clustering — Detailed Architecture

Hierarchical clustering requires two critical design decisions:

| Decision | Options | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| **Linkage Criterion** | Single / Complete / Average / Ward's | Defines how distance between two *clusters* is measured |
| **Distance Metric** | Euclidean / Manhattan / Cosine / Minkowski | Defines how distance between two *points* is measured |

### 2.1.1 Distance Metrics Between Two Points

Given two points $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n)$:

**Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ norm):**

$$d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - y_j)^2} = \vert\vert x - y \vert\vert_2$$

**Manhattan Distance ($L_1$ norm):**

$$d(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - y_j \vert = \vert\vert x - y \vert\vert_1$$

**Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ norm):**

$$d(x, y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p} = \vert\vert x - y \vert\vert_p$$

> [!NOTE]
> Euclidean is a special case of Minkowski with $p = 2$; Manhattan is the case $p = 1$.

### 2.1.2 Linkage Criteria — Distance Between Two Clusters

Let $C_a$ and $C_b$ be two clusters, and $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ the underlying point-wise distance.

| Linkage | Formula | Behaviour |
|---------|---------|-----------|
| **Single Linkage** (MIN) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min\limits_{x \in C_a,\, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce *long, chain-like* clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** (MAX) | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max\limits_{x \in C_a,\, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Tends to produce *compact, spherical* clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $d(C_a, C_b) = \dfrac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \,\vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a}\sum_{y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $\Delta(A, B) = \dfrac{\vert C_a \vert \,\vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \;\vert\vert \mu_a - \mu_b \vert\vert^2$ | Minimises the increase in total within-cluster SSE |

### 2.1.3 Agglomerative Algorithm — Formal Steps

For a dataset of $n$ points with desired number of clusters $k$:

- **Step 1:** Initialise $n$ singleton clusters, one per data point.
- **Step 2:** Compute the $n \times n$ **proximity matrix** $P$ of pairwise distances.
- **Step 3:** Repeat the following until only $k$ clusters remain:
    - Find the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ with the **smallest** distance per the chosen linkage.
    - **Merge** $C_i$ and $C_j$ into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
    - **Update** the proximity matrix by computing distances from $C_{ij}$ to every remaining cluster.
- **Step 4:** Return the cluster hierarchy (dendrogram).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Time Complexity:** Agglomerative clustering is $O(n^3)$ in the naive case and $O(n^2 \log n)$ with efficient priority queues — making it impractical for very large $n$.

---

## 2.2 Partitional Clustering — Theoretical Framework

The general optimisation problem for partitional clustering is:

$$\min_{\mathcal{C}} \;\; \mathcal{J}(\mathcal{C}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad \bigcup_{i=1}^{k} C_i = \mathcal{D}, \quad C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\;\forall\, i \neq j$$

where $\mathcal{C} = \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ is the partition and $\mathcal{J}$ is the chosen quality criterion (e.g., WCSS, silhouette, Davies-Bouldin Index).

### Comparison: Partitional vs Hierarchical

| Property | Partitional | Hierarchical |
|----------|-------------|--------------|
| **Structure** | Single flat partition | Nested tree (dendrogram) |
| **Pre-specify $k$** | Yes (required) | No (optional) |
| **Scalability** | High (e.g., K-Means $O(nkt)$) | Low ($O(n^2 \log n)$) |
| **Determinism** | Depends on initialisation | Fully deterministic |
| **Reversibility** | Can re-assign points | Cannot un-merge once done |

---

## 2.3 K-Means Clustering — Deep Mathematical Treatment

### 2.3.1 Objective Function (WCSS)

Given $k$ clusters $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k$ with centroids $\mu_1, \mu_2, \dots, \mu_k$, K-Means minimises:

$$J(\mathcal{C}, \mu) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert$ denotes the **Euclidean norm** of the difference vector — the squared Euclidean distance. This is the **Lloyd objective function**.

### 2.3.2 Centroid Update Rule

The optimal centroid of cluster $C_i$ that minimises the WCSS is the **arithmetic mean** of all points in $C_i$:

$$\mu_i \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$$

### 2.3.3 Cluster Assignment Rule

For a given centroid configuration, each point $x$ is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is **nearest** in Euclidean distance:

$$C(x) \;=\; \underset{i \in \{1, \dots, k\}}{\arg\min} \;\; \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^{2}$$

### 2.3.4 Convergence Guarantee

K-Means is guaranteed to **converge** in a finite number of iterations because:

- The WCSS $J$ is **bounded below** by $0$.
- Each iteration either decreases $J$ or leaves it unchanged.
- The number of distinct partitions is **finite** (at most $k^n$).

However, K-Means converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum.

### 2.3.5 Elbow Method for Choosing $k$

Plot $J(k)$ versus $k$ and identify the "elbow" — the point where the rate of decrease sharply shifts.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Default in K-Means |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | $L_1$ norm |
| 3 | Minkowski Distance | $d(x, y) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p}\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalised form |
| 4 | Single Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min d(x, y)$ | Chain effect |
| 5 | Complete Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max d(x, y)$ | Compact clusters |
| 6 | Average Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}\sum\sum d(x, y)$ | Balanced |
| 7 | Ward's Linkage | $\Delta = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \vert\vert \mu_a - \mu_b \vert\vert^2$ | Minimises SSE increase |
| 8 | K-Means WCSS | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ | Lloyd objective |
| 9 | Centroid | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert}\sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Mean of cluster |
| 10 | Assignment | $C(x) = \arg\min_i \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ | Nearest centroid |
| 11 | K-Means Complexity | $O(n \cdot k \cdot t \cdot d)$ | $t$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions |
| 12 | AGNES Complexity | $O(n^3)$ naive / $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimised | Hierarchical |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Use Case |
|--------|----------|
| **Image Segmentation** | Compressing image colour palettes via K-Means |
| **Customer Segmentation** | Marketing analytics, RFM-based grouping |
| **Anomaly Detection** | Outliers appear as far-from-centroid points |
| **Document Clustering** | Topic modelling, news article grouping |
| **Bioinformatics** | Gene expression clustering, protein family detection |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Cluster-head selection in LEACH protocol |
| **VLSI Design** | Circuit partitioning for layout optimisation |
| **Recommender Systems** | User behaviour clustering for collaborative filtering |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal Centroid

**Statement:** The centroid $\mu_i$ that minimises $\sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ for a fixed cluster $C_i$ is the arithmetic mean of $C_i$.

### Step 1: Set Up the Objective

Let $C_i$ contain $m$ points: $\{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \dots, x^{(m)}\}$. We wish to find the $\mu_i$ that minimises:

$$J(\mu_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \vert\vert x^{(j)} - \mu_i \vert\vert^{2}$$

### Step 2: Expand the Squared Norm

Using the identity $\vert\vert a - b \vert\vert^2 = (a - b)^\top (a - b) = \vert\vert a \vert\vert^2 - 2 a^\top b + \vert\vert b \vert\vert^2$:

$$J(\mu_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( \vert\vert x^{(j)} \vert\vert^{2} - 2\, (x^{(j)})^\top \mu_i + \vert\vert \mu_i \vert\vert^{2} \right)$$

### Step 3: Take the Gradient with Respect to $\mu_i$

$$\nabla_{\mu_i} J(\mu_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( -2 x^{(j)} + 2 \mu_i \right) \;=\; -2 \sum_{j=1}^{m} x^{(j)} + 2 m \mu_i$$

### Step 4: Set the Gradient to Zero

$$-2 \sum_{j=1}^{m} x^{(j)} + 2 m \mu_i \;=\; 0$$

### Step 5: Solve for $\mu_i$

$$2 m \mu_i \;=\; 2 \sum_{j=1}^{m} x^{(j)}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mu_i \;=\; \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} x^{(j)} \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x\;}$$

### Step 6: Confirm Second-Order Condition

$$\nabla^{2}_{\mu_i} J(\mu_i) \;=\; 2 m \, I \;\succ\; 0$$

Since the Hessian is a positive-definite scalar multiple of the identity, the critical point is indeed a **global minimum** for the within-cluster SSE of $C_i$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Detailed Worked Example — K-Means by Hand

**Problem:** Apply K-Means with $k = 2$ to the 2-D points: $A(1,1)$, $B(1,2)$, $C(2,1)$, $D(8,8)$, $E(9,9)$, $F(8,9)$.

### Iteration 1

**Step 1: Initialise centroids** (randomly). Let $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (2, 1)$.

**Step 2: Compute squared Euclidean distances** from each point to each centroid:

| Point | $\vert\vert x - \mu_1 \vert\vert^2$ | $\vert\vert x - \mu_2 \vert\vert^2$ | Cluster Assigned |
|-------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------|
| $A(1,1)$ | $0$ | $1$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(1,2)$ | $1$ | $2$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(2,1)$ | $1$ | $0$ | $C_2$ |
| $D(8,8)$ | $98$ | $90$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(9,9)$ | $128$ | $116$ | $C_2$ |
| $F(8,9)$ | $114$ | $100$ | $C_2$ |

**Resulting clusters:** $C_1 = \{A, B\}$ and $C_2 = \{C, D, E, F\}$.

**Step 3: Update centroids** as the means:

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{(1,1) + (1,2)}{2} = (1, 1.5)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{(2,1) + (8,8) + (9,9) + (8,9)}{4} = \left(\frac{27}{4}, \frac{27}{4}\right) = (6.75, 6.75)$$

### Iteration 2

**Step 1: Re-compute squared distances** from each point to the new centroids $\mu_1 = (1, 1.5)$ and $\mu_2 = (6.75, 6.75)$:

| Point | $\vert\vert x - \mu_1 \vert\vert^2$ | $\vert\vert x - \mu_2 \vert\vert^2$ | Cluster Assigned |
|-------|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|------------------|
| $A(1,1)$ | $0.25$ | $68.0625$ | $C_1$ |
| $B(1,2)$ | $0.25$ | $60.0625$ | $C_1$ |
| $C(2,1)$ | $1.25$ | $54.0625$ | $C_1$ |
| $D(8,8)$ | $49.25$ | $3.0625$ | $C_2$ |
| $E(9,9)$ | $70.25$ | $12.0625$ | $C_2$ |
| $F(8,9)$ | $61.25$ | $6.0625$ | $C_2$ |

**Resulting clusters:** $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$ and $C_2 = \{D, E, F\}$.

**Step 2: Recompute centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{(1,1) + (1,2) + (2,1)}{3} = \left(\frac{4}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) \approx (1.333, 1.333)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{(8,8) + (9,9) + (8,9)}{3} = \left(\frac{25}{3}, \frac{26}{3}\right) \approx (8.333, 8.667)$$

### Iteration 3

**Cluster assignments are unchanged:** $C_1 = \{A, B, C\}$, $C_2 = \{D, E, F\}$.

Since cluster membership did not change from Iteration 2 to Iteration 3, the algorithm has **converged**.

### Final Result

| Cluster | Members | Final Centroid |
|---------|---------|----------------|
| $C_1$ | $A(1,1), B(1,2), C(2,1)$ | $(1.333, 1.333)$ |
| $C_2$ | $D(8,8), E(9,9), F(8,9)$ | $(8.333, 8.667)$ |

### Final WCSS

$$J = \sum_{x \in C_1} \vert\vert x - \mu_1 \vert\vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \vert\vert x - \mu_2 \vert\vert^2$$

For $C_1$: $0.444 + 0.444 + 0.444 = 1.333$. For $C_2$: $0.222 + 0.222 + 0.222 = 0.667$. Total: $J = 2.0$.

---

## 3.3 Agglomerative Clustering — Worked Example

**Problem:** Cluster points $A(1,1)$, $B(1,2)$, $C(5,4)$, $D(5,5)$ using **single linkage** with Euclidean distance.

### Step 1: Initial Proximity Matrix

Compute pairwise Euclidean distances:

| | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ |
|---|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| $A$ | $0$ | $1.00$ | $5.00$ | $5.66$ |
| $B$ | $1.00$ | $0$ | $4.47$ | $5.00$ |
| $C$ | $5.00$ | $4.47$ | $0$ | $1.00$ |
| $D$ | $5.66$ | $5.00$ | $1.00$ | $0$ |

**Minimum distance:** $d(A, B) = 1.00$ and $d(C, D) = 1.00$. **Merge $\{A, B\}$** first.

### Step 2: Update Matrix

New clusters: $\{A, B\}$, $C$, $D$. For single linkage, recompute distances as the minimum:

| | $\{A,B\}$ | $C$ | $D$ |
|---|-----------|-----|-----|
| $\{A,B\}$ | $0$ | $4.47$ | $5.00$ |
| $C$ | $4.47$ | $0$ | $1.00$ |
| $D$ | $5.00$ | $1.00$ | $0$ |

**Minimum:** $d(C, D) = 1.00$. **Merge $\{C, D\}$.**

### Step 3: Final Merge

| | $\{A,B\}$ | $\{C,D\}$ |
|---|-----------|-----------|
| $\{A,B\}$ | $0$ | $4.47$ |
| $\{C,D\}$ | $4.47$ | $0$ |

**Merge into root cluster $\{A, B, C, D\}$.**

### Resulting Dendrogram (Schematic)

```
Level 4:    A   B   C   D
Level 3:     \ /     \ /
Level 2:     AB       CD
Level 1:        \   /
Level 0:          ABCD
```

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing All Three Methods

| Property | K-Means (Partitional) | Agglomerative (Hierarchical) |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Cluster Shape** | Spherical, equal-size | Arbitrary |
| **Pre-specify $k$** | Required | Optional |
| **Scalability** | Excellent $O(nkt)$ | Poor $O(n^2 \log n)$ |
| **Determinism** | Non-deterministic | Fully deterministic |
| **Sensitivity to Noise** | High | Moderate (depends on linkage) |
| **Visual Output** | Centroid plot | Dendrogram |
| **Clustering Quality** | Local optimum | Sub-optimal (greedy) |
| **Memory Footprint** | Stores centroids only | Stores full $n \times n$ matrix |
| **Best For** | Large $n$, known $k$ | Small $n$, unknown $k$ |
| **Reassignment** | Possible after each iteration | Not possible once merged |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Agglomerative Clustering — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START N Singleton Clusters"] --> B["Compute Full NxN Proximity Matrix"]
    B --> C["Find Two Closest Clusters Per Linkage Rule"]
    C --> D["Merge The Two Clusters Into One"]
    D --> E["Update Proximity Matrix"]
    E --> F{"Desired Number Of Clusters Reached OR Single Cluster"}
    F -->|NO| C
    F -->|YES| G["Cut Dendrogram At Chosen Height"]
    G --> H["OUTPUT Final K Clusters"]
```

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm — Iterative Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START Specify K And Input Dataset"] --> B["Initialise K Centroids Randomly Or Via K Means Plus Plus"]
    B --> C["ASSIGNMENT STEP Compute Distance From Each Point To All K Centroids"]
    C --> D["Assign Each Point To Nearest Centroid Cluster"]
    D --> E["UPDATE STEP Recompute Each Centroid As Mean Of Its Cluster Points"]
    E --> F["Compute WCSS Objective Function J"]
    F --> G{"Centroid Shift Below Threshold OR Max Iterations"}
    G -->|NO| C
    G -->|YES| H["OUTPUT Final K Centroids And Cluster Labels"]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical vs Partitional — Architecture Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph H["HIERARCHICAL CLUSTERING"]
        H1["Step 1 Singleton Clusters"]
        H2["Step 2 Pairwise Proximity"]
        H3["Step 3 Iterative Merging"]
        H4["Step 4 Output Dendrogram"]
        H1 --> H2 --> H3 --> H4
    end
    subgraph P["PARTITIONAL CLUSTERING K MEANS"]
        P1["Step 1 Choose K"]
        P2["Step 2 Initialise Centroids"]
        P3["Step 3 Iterative Assignment And Update"]
        P4["Step 4 Output Flat Partition"]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4
    end
```

## 4.4 Linkage Criterion — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DISTANCE BETWEEN CLUSTERS"] --> B["SINGLE LINKAGE"]
    A --> C["COMPLETE LINKAGE"]
    A --> D["AVERAGE LINKAGE"]
    A --> E["WARDS LINKAGE"]
    B --> B1["Minimum Pairwise Distance Produces Chain Like Clusters"]
    C --> C1["Maximum Pairwise Distance Produces Compact Clusters"]
    D --> D1["Mean Pairwise Distance Balanced Trade Off"]
    E --> E1["Minimises Within Cluster Variance Increase When Merged"]
```

## 4.5 K-Means Convergence — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Assign : Random Centroids
    Assign --> Update : Nearest Centroid Rule
    Update --> Check : Recompute Means
    Check --> Assign : Centroids Shifted
    Check --> [*] : Converged No Movement
```

## 4.6 Dendrogram Construction — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DATA POINTS A B C D E F"] --> B["COMPUTE NxN DISTANCE MATRIX"]
    B --> C["ITERATION 1 MERGE CLOSEST PAIR AB Height Equals Distance"]
    B --> D["ITERATION 1 MERGE SECOND PAIR DE Height Equals Distance"]
    C --> E["ITERATION 2 MERGE CD With EF Group Heights Updated"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["ITERATION 3 MERGE AB CD With EF HEIGHT Larger Value"]
    F --> G["ROOT CLUSTER ABCDEF AT TOP OF TREE"]
    G --> H["CUT AT CHOSEN HEIGHT H0 TO OBTAIN K CLUSTERS"]
```

## 4.7 K-Means — Error Minimisation Landscape

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STARTING CENTROIDS RANDOM"] --> B["COMPUTE WCSS J"]
    B --> C{"J Decreasing"}
    C -->|YES| D["Refine Centroid Position"]
    D --> B
    C -->|NO| E["Reached Local Minimum"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT Stable Cluster Assignment"]
```

## 4.8 Decision Tree — Which Clustering Algorithm To Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Need To Cluster Unlabelled Data"] --> B{"Is K Known"}
    B -->|YES| C{"Dataset Size"}
    B -->|NO| D["HIERARCHICAL CLUSTERING Build Dendrogram Cut For K"]
    C -->|Large N Greater Than 10K| E["K MEANS With K Means Plus Plus Initialisation"]
    C -->|Small N Less Than 10K| F{"Need Arbitrary Cluster Shapes"}
    F -->|YES| G["Agglomerative With Single Or Average Linkage"]
    F -->|NO| H["K MEANS Or Ward Agglomerative"]
```
