- Continues until every squad has been the test squad exactly once.
- Averages the five scores → this average is an honest estimate of how the method will perform on a brand-new class.

The teacher's "test score" generalises; **no student is used both for learning and grading simultaneously**, which prevents leakage.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Resampling

The following metrics are heavily tested in KTU examinations. Memorise their formulations:

- **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** $\mathrm{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$
- **RMSE:** $\mathrm{RMSE} = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}}$
- **Standard Error (Bootstrap):** $\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta})$
- **Bias (Bootstrap):** $\widehat{Bias}_B(\hat{\theta})$
- **Cross-Validation Estimate of Test Error:** $\mathrm{CV}_{(k)}$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Cognitive Level:** Understand (L2), Apply (L3), and Analyse (L4) — students must be able to *compute* the CV and Bootstrap estimates for small numeric examples given in the ESE question paper.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bootstrap — Theory of Operation

The bootstrap, introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)**, is a computationally intensive method that emulates the process of obtaining new samples from the population by repeatedly drawing samples **with replacement** from the original dataset.

### 2.1.1 Operational Steps

1. Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ denote the observed training dataset of size $n$.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$ (typically $B = 1000$ or $10000$):
   - Generate a **bootstrap sample** $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ by drawing $n$ observations uniformly at random from $\mathcal{D}$, **with replacement**.
   - Compute the statistic of interest: $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(\mathcal{D}^{*b})$.
3. Aggregate the $B$ bootstrap replicates to estimate the sampling distribution of $\hat{\theta}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "with replacement"?** If sampling were done *without replacement*, every bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ would simply be a permutation of $\mathcal{D}$. Replacement introduces the artificial variability that mimics population sampling.

### 2.1.2 Bootstrap Estimates

The four standard quantities derived from the bootstrap distribution $\{\hat{\theta}^{*1}, \hat{\theta}^{*2}, \ldots, \hat{\theta}^{*B}\}$ are:

- **Bootstrap Mean (Bias-Corrected Point Estimate):**
$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$$

- **Bootstrap Standard Error:**
$$\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\left(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*}\right)^2}$$

- **Bootstrap Bias Estimate:**
$$\widehat{Bias}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$$

- **Bootstrap Confidence Interval (Percentile Method):** For a $100(1-\alpha)\%$ CI,
$$CI_{(1-\alpha)} = \left[\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)} \;,\; \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}\right]$$
where $\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(q)}$ denotes the $q$-th quantile of the bootstrap distribution.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reliability Engineering:** Estimating the failure-rate confidence interval of mechanical components from limited test data.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Quantifying the variability of heart-rate variability (HRV) indices across patient cohorts.
- **Structural Health Monitoring:** Bootstrap estimates of modal frequencies where each measurement is expensive.

## 2.2 Cross-Validation — Theory of Operation

Cross-validation is a model-assessment technique that estimates the **test error rate** (generalisation error) by holding out portions of the training data during the fitting process.

### 2.2.1 The Validation Set Approach (Hold-Out)

The simplest variant: split $\mathcal{D}$ into:
- **Training set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{train}}$ (e.g., 70%) — used to fit the model.
- **Validation set** $\mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}$ (e.g., 30%) — used to estimate the test error.

$$\widehat{Err}_{\text{val}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{val}} \vert}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{D}_{\text{val}}}(y_i - \hat{f}(x_i))^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Drawback:** The validation estimate has high variance (depends on which observations land in the validation set) and overestimates the test error (model is trained on less data). KTU questions frequently ask students to identify these limitations.

### 2.2.2 k-Fold Cross-Validation

1. Randomly partition $\mathcal{D}$ into $k$ equally-sized (or nearly equal) disjoint folds: $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k$.
2. For fold $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:
   - Fit the model on $\mathcal{D} \setminus F_i$ (the union of the other $k-1$ folds).
   - Predict on the held-out fold $F_i$, producing predictions $\hat{y}_j$ for $j \in F_i$.
   - Compute $\mathrm{MSE}_i = \frac{1}{\vert F_i \vert}\sum_{j \in F_i}(y_j - \hat{y}_j)^2$.
3. The $k$-fold CV estimate is:
$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathrm{MSE}_i$$

**Common choices:**
- $k = 5$ or $k = 10$ are standard defaults in industry.
- $k = n$ is the special case of **Leave-One-Out CV (LOOCV)**.

### 2.2.3 Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)

Each fold contains exactly **one observation**:

$$CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$$

where $h_{ii}$ is the $i$-th diagonal element of the **hat matrix** $H = X(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top$ for linear models, which gives a closed-form shortcut.

### 2.2.4 Bias–Variance Trade-off in CV

| Property | LOOCV ($k=n$) | $k=10$ | Hold-out ($k=2$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bias of test-error estimate | **High** (nearly unbiased but uses $n-1$ for training) | Moderate | **High** (only 50% training) |
| Variance of test-error estimate | **Low** (averages over $n$ highly-correlated fits) | Moderate | **High** |
| Computational cost | **High** ($n$ fits) | Moderate | **Low** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Pitfall:** Do NOT claim LOOCV has lower bias than $k=10$. Both are (nearly) unbiased, but $k=5$ or $k=10$ has *lower* variance because the folds are less correlated. The Board awards marks for this nuance.

### 2.2.5 Stratified k-Fold (Briefly, for Classification)

For classification tasks, each fold preserves the class proportion of the original dataset. This is **Stratified k-Fold CV** and is the default in `scikit-learn` for classifiers.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Validation MSE | $\mathrm{MSE}_{\text{val}} = \frac{1}{n_{\text{val}}}\sum_{i \in \text{val}}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $n_{\text{val}} = \vert \mathcal{D}_{\text{val}} \vert$ |
| $k$-Fold CV | $CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathrm{MSE}_i$ | $k$ folds, $n_i \approx n/k$ per fold |
| LOOCV | $CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | $k=n$ |
| LOOCV (Linear shortcut) | $CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\dfrac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$ | $H = X(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top$ |
| Bootstrap Standard Error | $\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{B-1}\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2}$ | $B$ bootstrap replicates |
| Bootstrap Bias | $\widehat{Bias}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ | $\hat{\theta} = s(\mathcal{D})$ |
| Percentile CI | $CI_{(1-\alpha)} = \left[\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)}, \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}\right]$ | Quantile-based |
| Hat Matrix Trace | $\sum_{i=1}^{n}h_{ii} = \text{tr}(H) = p+1$ | $p$ = number of predictors |

> [!NOTE]
> The trace identity $\sum_i h_{ii} = p+1$ is the basis for the **generalised** CV (GCV) formula $GCV = \dfrac{\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{(1 - (p+1)/n)^2}$, which occasionally appears in advanced ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Bootstrap Standard Error by Hand (KTU 3-Mark Favourite)

**Problem:** Given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{2, 4, 6, 8\}$ and the bootstrap replicate statistics $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ for $B = 5$ bootstrap samples as listed below, compute the **bootstrap standard error** of the sample mean.

| $b$ | $\mathcal{D}^{*b}$ | $\hat{\theta}^{*b}$ (mean) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\{2, 2, 6, 8\}$ | $4.50$ |
| 2 | $\{4, 4, 8, 8\}$ | $6.00$ |
| 3 | $\{2, 4, 6, 6\}$ | $4.50$ |
| 4 | $\{2, 4, 8, 8\}$ | $5.50$ |
| 5 | $\{6, 6, 8, 8\}$ | $7.00$ |

**Step 1: Compute the bootstrap mean $\bar{\theta}^{*}$.**

$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}\hat{\theta}^{*b}$$

$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{4.50 + 6.00 + 4.50 + 5.50 + 7.00}{5}$$

$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{27.50}{5} = 5.50$$

**Step 2: Compute squared deviations from $\bar{\theta}^{*}$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
(\hat{\theta}^{*1} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 &= (4.50 - 5.50)^2 = (-1.00)^2 = 1.00 \\
(\hat{\theta}^{*2} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 &= (6.00 - 5.50)^2 = (0.50)^2 = 0.25 \\
(\hat{\theta}^{*3} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 &= (4.50 - 5.50)^2 = (-1.00)^2 = 1.00 \\
(\hat{\theta}^{*4} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 &= (5.50 - 5.50)^2 = (0.00)^2 = 0.00 \\
(\hat{\theta}^{*5} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 &= (7.00 - 5.50)^2 = (1.50)^2 = 2.25
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Sum and divide by $B-1$ (Bessel's correction for an unbiased sample variance).**

$$\sum_{b=1}^{B}(\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2 = 1.00 + 0.25 + 1.00 + 0.00 + 2.25 = 4.50$$

$$s^{*2} = \frac{4.50}{5 - 1} = \frac{4.50}{4} = 1.125$$

**Step 4: Take the square root.**

$$\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{1.125} \approx 1.061$$

**Verification against classical formula:** The classical SE of the sample mean for $\mathcal{D} = \{2,4,6,8\}$ is $\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 2.582 / 2 = 1.291$ (using sample SD). The bootstrap SE of $1.061$ is in the same ballpark; small discrepancy is expected with only $B=5$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — 5-Fold Cross-Validation (KTU 14-Mark Favourite)

**Problem:** A regression dataset of $n=10$ observations is split into $k=5$ folds. Compute $CV_{(5)}$ given the per-fold MSE values:

| Fold $i$ | $\mathrm{MSE}_i$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | $2.10$ |
| 2 | $1.85$ |
| 3 | $2.45$ |
| 4 | $1.95$ |
| 5 | $2.25$ |

**Step 1: Identify the $k$-fold CV formula.**

$$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathrm{MSE}_i$$

**Step 2: Substitute $k = 5$.**

$$CV_{(5)} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}\mathrm{MSE}_i$$

**Step 3: Compute the sum.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5}\mathrm{MSE}_i = 2.10 + 1.85 + 2.45 + 1.95 + 2.25 = 10.60$$

**Step 4: Divide by $k$.**

$$CV_{(5)} = \frac{10.60}{5} = 2.12$$

**Interpretation:** The estimated test MSE is **2.12**. If a competing model yields $CV_{(5)} = 1.87$, the second model is preferred under MSE assessment (lower is better).

## 3.3 LOOCV — Linear-Model Shortcut Derivation

For a linear regression $\hat{y} = X\hat{\beta} = Hy$, where $H = X(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top$ is the idempotent hat matrix (i.e., $H^2 = H$, $H^\top = H$).

**Starting point:** Fit the model on $\mathcal{D} \setminus \{i\}$ to obtain $\hat{\beta}^{(i)}$, and predict $y_i$. The LOOCV residual is:

$$e^{(i)} = y_i - x_i^\top \hat{\beta}^{(i)}$$

**Key identity (without proof — derive from normal equations):**

$$\hat{\beta}^{(i)} = \hat{\beta} - \frac{(X^\top X)^{-1}x_i \, e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

where $e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i$ is the $i$-th residual from the full-data fit.

**Substituting:**

$$e^{(i)} = y_i - x_i^\top \left[\hat{\beta} - \frac{(X^\top X)^{-1}x_i e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right]$$

$$e^{(i)} = y_i - x_i^\top \hat{\beta} + \frac{x_i^\top(X^\top X)^{-1}x_i \, e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

Using $x_i^\top(X^\top X)^{-1}x_i = h_{ii}$ and $y_i - x_i^\top\hat{\beta} = e_i$:

$$e^{(i)} = e_i + \frac{h_{ii}\, e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

$$e^{(i)} = e_i \left[\frac{1 - h_{ii} + h_{ii}}{1 - h_{ii}}\right] = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Instead of fitting the model $n$ times (expensive), you fit it **once** and divide each residual by $(1-h_{ii})$. This is a frequently asked 14-mark derivation in the KTU ESE.

## 3.4 Bias-Variance Decomposition — Analytical Matrix

The expected prediction error of a model at input $x_0$ decomposes as:

$$\mathrm{Err}(x_0) = \mathrm{Irreducible\ Error} + \mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance}$$

$$\mathrm{Err}(x_0) = \sigma_\varepsilon^2 + \left[\mathbb{E}\hat{f}(x_0) - f(x_0)\right]^2 + \mathbb{E}\left[\hat{f}(x_0) - \mathbb{E}\hat{f}(x_0)\right]^2$$

| CV Variant | Effect on Bias of Test-Error Estimate | Effect on Variance of Test-Error Estimate |
|---|---|---|
| Hold-out ($k=2$) | High (underestimates because model trains on less data) | High (only 1 split) |
| $k=10$ | Moderate | Moderate |
| LOOCV ($k=n$) | Low (nearly unbiased, $n-1$ training) | High (averages $n$ highly-correlated fits) |
| Repeated $k$-Fold | Low | **Lowest** (average over multiple splits) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In practice, $k=10$ repeated 5–10 times is the empirical sweet spot — a fact students often cite for **2 bonus marks** in viva voce.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cross-Validation Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Dataset D of size n] --> B[Random Shuffle of Indices]
    B --> C1[Fold 1: indices 1 to n/k]
    B --> C2[Fold 2: indices n/k+1 to 2n/k]
    B --> C3[Fold 3: indices 2n/k+1 to 3n/k]
    B --> C4[Fold 4: indices 3n/k+1 to 4n/k]
    B --> C5[Fold 5: indices 4n/k+1 to n]
    C1 --> D1[Iteration i = 1]
    C2 --> D2[Iteration i = 2]
    C3 --> D3[Iteration i = 3]
    C4 --> D4[Iteration i = 4]
    C5 --> D5[Iteration i = 5]
    D1 --> E1[Train on F2 U F3 U F4 U F5]
    D2 --> E2[Train on F1 U F3 U F4 U F5]
    D3 --> E3[Train on F1 U F2 U F4 U F5]
    D4 --> E4[Train on F1 U F2 U F3 U F5]
    D5 --> E5[Train on F1 U F2 U F3 U F4]
    E1 --> F1[Predict on F1: compute MSE1]
    E2 --> F2[Predict on F2: compute MSE2]
    E3 --> F3[Predict on F3: compute MSE3]
    E4 --> F4[Predict on F4: compute MSE4]
    E5 --> F5[Predict on F5: compute MSE5]
    F1 --> G[Sum MSEi over all k folds]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Divide by k equals 5]
    H --> I[CV5 equals 0.2 x sum of MSEi]
    I --> J[Final CV Estimate of Test Error]
```

## 4.2 Bootstrap Resampling Process (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Dataset D = x1 to xn] --> B{For b = 1 to B}
    B --> C[Generate Bootstrap Sample Dstar b]
    C --> D[Draw n observations with replacement from D]
    D --> E[Compute Statistic thetastar b on Dstar b]
    E --> F[Store thetastar b in array]
    F --> B
    B --> G[End loop after B replicates]
    G --> H[Compute Bootstrap Mean thetabar star]
    G --> I[Compute Bootstrap SE = sqrt of sample variance]
    G --> J[Compute Bootstrap Bias = thetabar star minus theta hat]
    G --> K[Construct Percentile CI from quantiles]
    H --> L[Final Bootstrap Inference Report]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Comparison: Bootstrap vs Cross-Validation (Mermaid Quadrant View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ResamplingParadigm
        direction LR
        subgraph BootstrapBranch
            B1[Goal: Quantify uncertainty] --> B2[Sample WITH replacement] --> B3[Output: SE, Bias, CI] --> B4[Best for: parameter inference]
        end
        subgraph CrossValBranch
            C1[Goal: Estimate test error] --> C2[Sample WITHOUT replacement] --> C3[Output: CVk, model selection] --> C4[Best for: model assessment]
        end
    end
    B4 -.shared concept.-> C4
```

## 4.4 Leave-One-Out CV Special Case (Mermaid Linear Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dataset D with n samples] --> B[Hold out sample i]
    B --> C[Train on n-1 samples]
    C --> D[Predict held-out sample i]
    D --> E[Compute residual e_i divided by 1 minus h_ii]
    E --> F[Store CV residual]
    F --> G{All i = 1 to n done?}
    G -- No --> B
    G -- Yes --> H[Average squared CV residuals]
    H --> I[Output: LOOCV estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> All four diagrams are designed to be directly **drawn on the answer script** during an ESE examination. The Mermaid source above is for instructor reference; the textual node descriptions can be transcribed as labeled boxes connected by arrows.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]
**Define the Bootstrap method. List any two limitations of the Bootstrap.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Bootstrap is a resampling technique introduced by Bradley Efron (1979) in which multiple "pseudo-samples" (called **bootstrap samples**) are generated by drawing observations **with replacement** from the original dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$. A statistic $\hat{\theta}^{*b} = s(\mathcal{D}^{*b})$ is computed on each bootstrap sample, and the empirical distribution of $\{\hat{\theta}^{*b}\}_{b=1}^{B}$ is used to estimate the **standard error, bias, and confidence intervals** of $\hat{\theta}$.
>
> **Limitations (1 mark, any two):**
> 1. **Computationally expensive** for large $n$ and large $B$ (e.g., $B=10{,}000$ replicates).
> 2. Assumes the observed sample is **representative** of the population; biased if the original sample is biased.
> 3. **Fails for extreme-order statistics** (e.g., the sample maximum), as the bootstrap sample maximum is bounded by the observed maximum.
> 4. **Not suitable for dependent data** (time series, spatial data) without modification (e.g., block bootstrap).

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]
**Explain the difference between the validation set approach and $k$-fold cross-validation.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> | Aspect | Validation Set Approach | $k$-Fold Cross-Validation |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Data Split** | One random split into 2 parts (e.g., 70/30). | $k$ disjoint folds; each fold used once as test. |
> | **Bias of Error Estimate** | Can be **high** (model trained on less data). | **Lower** (model trained on $(k-1)n/k$ data). |
> | **Variance of Error Estimate** | Can be **high** (single split). | **Lower** (averaged over $k$ splits). |
> | **Computation** | Train once. | Train $k$ times. |
>
> (Marks awarded for: correct distinction + 2 differentiating points.)

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **$k$-fold cross-validation** procedure in detail. Discuss the **bias–variance trade-off** for the choices $k=2$ (hold-out), $k=5$ or $k=10$, and $k=n$ (LOOCV).

**(b) [7 marks]** Given the dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{3, 5, 7, 9, 11\}$ (size $n=5$), perform **Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)** for a simple linear regression model $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ fitted using ordinary least squares. Compute $CV_{(5)}$ from the predicted residuals. **Assume** the model $\hat{y} = 1 + 0.8 x$ is fit on the training subset each time (i.e., use this closed-form prediction equation; no need to refit).

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **[1 mark]** State the $k$-fold CV algorithm step-by-step (as in §2.2.2 above).
2. **[1 mark]** Define the CV formula $CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k}\mathrm{MSE}_i$.
3. **[3 marks]** Discuss bias-variance for each $k$:
   - **Hold-out ($k=2$):** High bias (only 50% training data → under-fit risk); high variance (single split).
   - **$k=5$ or $k=10$:** Moderate bias (90% training); moderate variance (averaged over $k$ uncorrelated folds).
   - **LOOCV ($k=n$):** Low bias (99% training when $n$ large); high variance (fold predictions are highly correlated because training sets differ by only one observation).
4. **[1 mark]** Conclude with the empirical recommendation: $k=10$ repeated 5 times is the de-facto industry standard.
5. **[1 mark]** Note LOOCV has a closed-form shortcut for linear models using the hat matrix.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

The training set has $n=4$ points and we use $\hat{y} = 1 + 0.8 x$.

**Step 1: Hold out $x_1 = 3$, predict $\hat{y}_1 = 1 + 0.8(3) = 1 + 2.4 = 3.4$.**

$$e_1 = y_1 - \hat{y}_1 = 3 - 3.4 = -0.4$$

**Step 2: Hold out $x_2 = 5$, predict $\hat{y}_2 = 1 + 0.8(5) = 1 + 4.0 = 5.0$.**

$$e_2 = 5 - 5.0 = 0$$

**Step 3: Hold out $x_3 = 7$, predict $\hat{y}_3 = 1 + 0.8(7) = 1 + 5.6 = 6.6$.**

$$e_3 = 7 - 6.6 = 0.4$$

**Step 4: Hold out $x_4 = 9$, predict $\hat{y}_4 = 1 + 0.8(9) = 1 + 7.2 = 8.2$.**

$$e_4 = 9 - 8.2 = 0.8$$

**Step 5: Hold out $x_5 = 11$, predict $\hat{y}_5 = 1 + 0.8(11) = 1 + 8.8 = 9.8$.**

$$e_5 = 11 - 9.8 = 1.2$$

**Step 6: Compute the LOOCV estimate.**

$$CV_{(5)} = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5}e_i^2 = \frac{(-0.4)^2 + 0^2 + 0.4^2 + 0.8^2 + 1.2^2}{5}$$

$$CV_{(5)} = \frac{0.16 + 0 + 0.16 + 0.64 + 1.44}{5} = \frac{2.40}{5} = 0.48$$

**Final Answer (boxed):** $\boxed{CV_{(5)} = 0.48}$

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **Bootstrap** method for estimating the standard error of an estimator. State and explain the **four bootstrap estimates**: mean, standard error, bias, and percentile confidence interval.

**(b) [7 marks]** A dataset has $B = 4$ bootstrap replicates of a correlation coefficient: $\hat{\theta}^{*1} = 0.42$, $\hat{\theta}^{*2} = 0.55$, $\hat{\theta}^{*3} = 0.48$, $\hat{\theta}^{*4} = 0.65$. The original sample correlation is $\hat{\theta} = 0.50$. Compute:
- (i) Bootstrap mean $\bar{\theta}^{*}$
- (ii) Bootstrap standard error $\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta})$
- (iii) Bootstrap bias $\widehat{Bias}_B(\hat{\theta})$
- (iv) Approximate 95% bootstrap confidence interval (using the empirical quantiles).

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **[2 marks]** Define Bootstrap and Efron's motivation (estimate $F$, the true CDF).
2. **[1 mark]** Algorithm: sample with replacement, compute statistic, repeat.
3. **[1 mark]** Bootstrap mean formula.
4. **[1 mark]** Bootstrap SE formula (with $B-1$ denominator).
5. **[1 mark]** Bootstrap bias formula.
6. **[1 mark]** Percentile CI formula and interpretation.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Bootstrap mean [2 marks]:**

$$\bar{\theta}^{*} = \frac{0.42 + 0.55 + 0.48 + 0.65}{4} = \frac{2.10}{4} = 0.525$$

**(ii) Bootstrap SE [3 marks]:**

Squared deviations:

$$\begin{aligned}
(0.42 - 0.525)^2 &= (-0.105)^2 = 0.011025 \\
(0.55 - 0.525)^2 &= (0.025)^2 = 0.000625 \\
(0.48 - 0.525)^2 &= (-0.045)^2 = 0.002025 \\
(0.65 - 0.525)^2 &= (0.125)^2 = 0.015625
\end{aligned}$$

Sum: $0.011025 + 0.000625 + 0.002025 + 0.015625 = 0.029300$

$$\widehat{SE}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \sqrt{\frac{0.029300}{4-1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.029300}{3}} = \sqrt{0.009767} \approx 0.0988$$

**(iii) Bootstrap bias [1 mark]:**

$$\widehat{Bias}_B(\hat{\theta}) = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta} = 0.525 - 0.50 = 0.025$$

**Interpretation:** The bootstrap indicates the original estimator is biased *upward* by approximately 0.025.

**(iv) 95% Bootstrap CI [1 mark]:**

With only $B=4$, the empirical $\alpha/2 = 0.025$ and $1-\alpha/2 = 0.975$ quantiles are approximated by the order statistics. Sorted: $\{0.42, 0.48, 0.55, 0.65\}$. Using the percentile method:

$$CI_{95\%} \approx [0.42,\ 0.65]$$

(Specifying that this is a coarse approximation due to small $B$ — real applications use $B \geq 1000$.)

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Triggers in OECST614 Resampling Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "with replacement" with "without replacement":** Bootstrap MUST use replacement. Cross-validation MUST NOT. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this is reversed.
> 2. **Forgetting the $B-1$ denominator:** Sample variance uses $B-1$, not $B$. Missing this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $k=2$ and claiming "low bias":** Hold-out ($k=2$) has HIGH bias because only 50% data is used for training. Many students write the opposite.
> 4. **Saying LOOCV has the lowest variance:** It has the LOWEST bias but HIGHEST variance. The exam answer must be precise.
> 5. **Mixing up Bias and SE formulas:** Bootstrap bias = $\bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$ (subtraction in that order). SE requires the SQUARE ROOT of the variance.
> 6. **Neglecting to interpret the CV/Bootstrap result:** A numerical answer without one line of interpretation loses 1 mark per sub-part.
> 7. **Citing the wrong author:** The Bootstrap was introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)** — not Breiman, Hastie, or Tibshirani (who wrote ESL but did not invent Bootstrap).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This Before ESE):**

- [x] **Bootstrap = with replacement; CV = without replacement.**
- [x] **Bootstrap estimates SE, bias, and CI of a parameter.**
- [x] **CV estimates the test (generalisation) error of a model.**
- [x] **Recommended $B$ for Bootstrap:** $B = 1000$–$10{,}000$.
- [x] **Recommended $k$ for CV:** $k = 5$ or $k = 10$; repeated 5–10 times in practice.
- [x] **LOOCV = $k = n$**, has the lowest bias but highest variance.
- [x] **LOOCV shortcut (linear models):** $CV_{(n)} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}}\right)^2$.
- [x] **Hat-matrix trace:** $\sum_i h_{ii} = p+1$ → basis of GCV.
- [x] **Bootstrap bias formula:** $\widehat{Bias}_B = \bar{\theta}^{*} - \hat{\theta}$.
- [x] **Bootstrap SE formula:** $\widehat{SE}_B = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1}\sum_b (\hat{\theta}^{*b} - \bar{\theta}^{*})^2}$.
- [x] **Percentile CI:** $CI_{(1-\alpha)} = [\hat{\theta}^{*}_{(\alpha/2)},\ \hat{\theta}^{*}_{(1-\alpha/2)}]$.
- [x] **Stratified $k$-Fold** preserves class proportions — essential for imbalanced classification.
- [x] **Time-series data** requires **forward-chaining** CV, NOT random $k$-Fold (data leakage).
- [x] **Bootstrap fails** for extreme order statistics and dependent data → use **block bootstrap** for the latter.
- [x] **Cross-validation is an *assessment* technique, not an *optimisation* technique** — once $CV_{(k)}$ is computed, retrain on the full dataset for final deployment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Aspects - Bias-Variance Trade-off
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST614 - MACHINE LEARNING FOR ENGINEERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Practical Aspects - Bias-Variance Trade-off

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In supervised learning, the **Bias-Variance Trade-off** is the foundational framework used to diagnose and decompose the *expected generalization error* of a learning algorithm. It quantitatively dissects the prediction error into three structurally distinct sources: the **Bias** (systematic error due to wrong assumptions), the **Variance** (sensitivity to fluctuations in the training set), and the **Irreducible Error** (noise inherent in the data).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 4 of OECST614 places strong emphasis on the *practical* aspects of model selection. The bias-variance trade-off is the cornerstone of understanding why a model that achieves 100% training accuracy may catastrophically fail on unseen data.

### 1.2 The Archery Analogy — Intuition First

Imagine four archers shooting at a bullseye. Each archer shoots a fixed number of arrows, then we plot where the arrows land.

| Archetype | Bias | Variance | Visual Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tight Cluster, Off-Center** | High | Low | Arrows land together, but far from bullseye |
| **Scattered, On Average Centered** | Low | High | Arrows spread out, but average lands on bullseye |
| **Tight Cluster, On Bullseye** | Low | Low | The ideal learning algorithm |
| **Scattered, Off-Center** | High | High | Worst case — underfits and overfits simultaneously |

In Machine Learning:
- **Bias** = the error introduced by approximating a complex real-world problem with a *simplified* model (e.g., fitting a straight line to a curved dataset).
- **Variance** = the amount by which our model's prediction would change if we estimated it using a *different training dataset*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Intuition:** Increasing model complexity → **Bias decreases** but **Variance increases**. The art of machine learning is finding the *sweet spot* that minimizes total error.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants)

The universally accepted scalar metric for regression in this context is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, mathematically defined as:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

The expected prediction error at point $x$ is denoted as $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2]$, which serves as the anchor for the entire bias-variance decomposition theorem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underfitting–Overfitting Spectrum

A model can be characterized by where it sits on the *complexity axis*:

- **Underfitting (High Bias, Low Variance):** The model is too simple. It cannot capture the underlying trend of the data. Symptoms include *high training error* and *high test error* that are nearly equal.
- **Overfitting (Low Bias, High Variance):** The model is too complex. It memorizes training data, including its noise. Symptoms include *very low training error* but *high test error*.
- **Good Fit (Balanced):** The model captures the signal and ignores the noise. Training and test errors are low and close to each other.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Decomposition

Consider a regression problem where the true relationship is $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, with $\varepsilon$ being zero-mean noise with variance $\sigma^2$.

1. **Pick a test point $x$**: We want to know how badly our learned model $\hat{f}(x)$ will err on average over all possible training sets drawn from the same distribution.
2. **Compute expected squared error**: Take the expectation $\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$.
3. **Expand the square**: Use the identity $(a - b)^2 = (a - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])^2 + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - b)^2 + 2(a - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}])(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}] - b)$.
4. **Take expectations**: The cross-term vanishes because $y$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ given $x$, and $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$.
5. **Identify three components**: A squared bias term, a variance term, and the irreducible noise.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master This Table — It Appears in Nearly Every KTU Board Exam on This Topic.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected Test MSE | $\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$ | Total generalization error at $x$ |
| 2 | Bias² | $\left(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)\right)^2$ | Error from wrong model assumptions |
| 3 | Variance | $\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$ | Sensitivity to training data sampling |
| 4 | Irreducible Error | $\sigma^2 = \mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon)$ | Noise floor — cannot be reduced |
| 5 | Master Decomposition | $\mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | The three-way split of MSE |
| 6 | Training Error Behavior | Decreases monotonically with complexity | A diagnostic signal |
| 7 | Validation Error Behavior | U-shaped curve vs. complexity | A diagnostic signal |
| 8 | Total Error (Sum) | $\mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | What we ultimately minimize |

> **Note on Notation:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ is intentionally avoided; the squared-bias expression uses parentheses with an explicit superscript rather than vertical bars, preserving the markdown table structure.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In production ML systems (e.g., medical diagnosis, autonomous driving, financial forecasting), the bias-variance trade-off directly governs:
- **Model Selection:** Choosing between a linear regressor, a decision tree, or a deep neural network.
- **Hyperparameter Tuning:** Selecting tree depth, regularization strength $\lambda$, or the number of hidden units.
- **Data Strategy:** Deciding whether to collect more data (reduces variance) or engineer better features (reduces bias).
- **Ensemble Methods:** Bagging reduces variance; Boosting reduces bias. The trade-off justifies their coexistence.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bias-Variance Decomposition

**Setup.** Let $x$ be a fixed test point. The true label is generated as $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, where $\varepsilon$ is independent noise with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$. The model trained on dataset $\mathcal{D}$ produces prediction $\hat{f}(x) = \hat{f}(x; \mathcal{D})$.

**Goal.** Show that the expected squared prediction error decomposes into three terms.

**Step 1.** Start with the definition of expected squared error at point $x$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

**Step 2.** Add and subtract the expected prediction $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the square, using the algebraic identity $a - c = (a - b) + (b - c)$:

$$y - \hat{f}(x) = (y - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]) + (\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))$$

**Step 3.** Substitute and square the expression:

$$(y - \hat{f}(x))^2 = (y - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + (\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))^2 + 2(y - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])(\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))$$

**Step 4.** Take expectation over both the noise $\varepsilon$ and the training set $\mathcal{D}$. The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}[(y - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])(\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))] = 0$. The reason: given $x$, $y$ depends on $\varepsilon$ only, while the second factor depends on $\mathcal{D}$ only, and these are independent.

**Step 5.** Analyze the first squared term. Since $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}\left[(y - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right] = \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right]$$

**Step 6.** Split this into two pieces using $(p + q)^2 = p^2 + q^2 + 2pq$ and noting that $f(x)$ and $\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)]$ are constants w.r.t. $\varepsilon$:

$$= (f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + \mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\varepsilon^2] + 2(f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])\mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\varepsilon]$$

**Step 7.** Since $\mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\varepsilon] = 0$, the cross-term drops. Since $\mathbb{E}_{\varepsilon}[\varepsilon^2] = \mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$:

$$\text{First term} = (f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + \sigma^2$$

**Step 8.** Analyze the second squared term. By the definition of variance:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}\left[(\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathrm{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}(\hat{f}(x))$$

**Step 9.** Assemble the final identity:

$$\boxed{\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}, \varepsilon}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \underbrace{(f(x) - \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}}[\hat{f}(x)])^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathrm{Var}_{\mathcal{D}}(\hat{f}(x))}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Error}}}$$

### 3.2 Numerical Example — Verifying the Decomposition

> [!NOTE]
> This is the type of step-by-step numerical problem KTU examiners love to set as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Problem:** A regression model is trained on three different datasets, all of size $n = 50$, sampled from the same distribution. At a particular test point $x_0$, the true value is $f(x_0) = 5.0$, and the noise variance is $\sigma^2 = 0.25$. The three model predictions are $\hat{f}_1(x_0) = 4.6$, $\hat{f}_2(x_0) = 5.4$, $\hat{f}_3(x_0) = 4.8$.

**Step A — Compute the mean prediction:**

$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)] \approx \frac{4.6 + 5.4 + 4.8}{3} = \frac{14.8}{3} \approx 4.933$$

**Step B — Compute the Bias²:**

$$\mathrm{Bias}^2 = (f(x_0) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x_0)])^2 = (5.0 - 4.933)^2 = (0.067)^2 \approx 0.00449$$

**Step C — Compute the Variance:**

$$\mathrm{Var} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(4.6 - 4.933)^2 + (5.4 - 4.933)^2 + (4.8 - 4.933)^2\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.1109 + 0.2180 + 0.0177\right] = \frac{0.3466}{3} \approx 0.1155$$

**Step D — Add the Irreducible Error:**

$$\sigma^2 = 0.25$$

**Step E — Total Expected MSE:**

$$\text{Total} = 0.00449 + 0.1155 + 0.25 \approx 0.3700$$

**Step F — Interpretation:** Even with a well-centered mean prediction (very low bias), the model's high variance dominates the reducible error. The model is *overfitting* — it is unstable across training sets. The fix is to introduce regularization or use a simpler hypothesis class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Error Decomposition Flow

The following diagram maps the *flow* by which the total expected error at a test point is generated from its three constituent sources. This is a Mermaid block diagram (not a physical drawing), as per KTU protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Dataset D] --> B[Learning Algorithm]
    B --> C[Model Prediction fhat]
    D[True Function f at point x] --> E[Systematic Gap]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Bias Component]
    A --> G[Dataset Sampling Variability]
    G --> H[Variance Component]
    C --> H
    I[Data Noise epsilon] --> J[Irreducible Error sigma squared]
    F --> K[Total Expected MSE]
    H --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Generalization Error Diagnostic]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow: Model Complexity vs. Error Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Complexity Model] --> A1[High Bias]
    A --> A2[Low Variance]
    A --> A3[Underfitting]
    B[Optimal Complexity Sweet Spot] --> B1[Balanced Bias]
    B --> B2[Balanced Variance]
    B --> B3[Minimum Total Error]
    C[High Complexity Model] --> C1[Low Bias]
    C --> C2[High Variance]
    C --> C3[Overfitting]
    A1 --> D[Total Error Curve]
    A2 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[U-shaped Validation Error]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart for Diagnosing Model Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Model Trained] --> Q1{Training Error Low?}
    Q1 -- No --> Underfit[Diagnosis: Underfitting - High Bias]
    Underfit --> R1[Action: Increase model complexity or add features]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Test Error Also Low?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Good[Diagnosis: Good Fit - Low Bias, Low Variance]
    Good --> R2[Action: Deploy and monitor]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Large Gap Between Train and Test Error?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Overfit[Diagnosis: Overfitting - High Variance]
    Overfit --> R3[Action: Regularization, more data, simpler model]
    Q3 -- No --> Mixed[Diagnosis: Mixed Bias and Variance]
    Mixed --> R4[Action: Feature engineering and regularization]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the KTU board exam, a labeled block diagram explaining the *flow* of how bias, variance, and noise combine to form total error is treated as equivalent to (and sometimes superior to) a hand-drawn chart. Mermaid-style block diagrams are fully acceptable in script-based answers.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the terms 'Bias' and 'Variance' in the context of supervised learning. How are they related to model complexity?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Bias (1 mark):** The error introduced by approximating a real-world problem, which may be extremely complex, with a simplified model. It quantifies how far the *average* prediction of the model is from the true function. Mathematically, $\mathrm{Bias}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$.
- **Variance (1 mark):** The amount by which the model's prediction would change if we trained it on a different training set. It quantifies the *sensitivity* of the model to fluctuations in the sample data. Mathematically, $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) = \mathbb{E}[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2]$.
- **Relation to Complexity (1 mark):** As model complexity increases, bias typically *decreases* (the model can fit more intricate patterns) while variance typically *increases* (the model becomes more sensitive to training data noise). This inverse relationship is the essence of the bias-variance trade-off.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the 'Irreducible Error'? Can it be eliminated by improving the model? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The irreducible error, denoted $\sigma^2$, is the variance of the noise term $\varepsilon$ in the data-generating process $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$ **(1 mark)**. It represents the inherent randomness or uncertainty in the target variable that cannot be captured by *any* model, no matter how sophisticated **(1 mark)**. It cannot be eliminated by improving the model because it stems from the data itself (e.g., measurement noise, unobserved variables, intrinsic stochasticity). Even a perfect oracle that knows $f(x)$ exactly will still have expected squared error equal to $\sigma^2$ on average **(1 mark)**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Module 4 questions in ESE offer an internal choice. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the bias-variance decomposition of expected prediction error. Derive the mathematical identity:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance} + \sigma^2$$

State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how the bias-variance trade-off influences model selection in practice. Explain with examples how the following techniques address the trade-off: (i) Regularization, (ii) Cross-validation, (iii) Ensemble methods (Bagging vs Boosting). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Draw (or describe) a flow showing: *True function $f(x)$* and *Model prediction $\hat{f}(x)$* feeding into a *Systematic Gap* block, which together with *Dataset Sampling Noise* and *Data Noise* produces *Total Expected MSE*. The diagram should clearly show the three inputs converging to the total error.

**Mathematical Derivation (4 marks):** The derivation is presented step-by-step below.

Let $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, with $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\mathrm{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^2$.

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[(f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^2\right]$$

Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$ inside the square:

$$= \mathbb{E}\left[\left((f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]) + (\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)) + \varepsilon\right)^2\right]$$

Expanding the square and taking expectations, the cross-terms vanish because $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}$ and has zero mean:

$$= (f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2 + \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^2\right] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^2]$$

Recognizing each piece:

$$= \mathrm{Bias}^2(\hat{f}(x)) + \mathrm{Var}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^2$$

**Term Meanings (1 mark):**
- $\mathrm{Bias}^2$ = systematic error from wrong model assumptions.
- $\mathrm{Variance}$ = sensitivity to the specific training set used.
- $\sigma^2$ = irreducible noise in the data.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks:**

The bias-variance trade-off means that no single model is universally best; the practitioner's job is to locate the *sweet spot* on the complexity axis **(1 mark)**.

**(i) Regularization (2 marks):** Adds a penalty term (e.g., $\lambda \sum \theta_j^2$ in Ridge regression) to the loss function. This forces the model to keep parameters small, effectively *constraining complexity*. It is a direct tool for reducing variance at the cost of a slight increase in bias — a deliberate trade-off.

**(ii) Cross-validation (2 marks):** Provides an empirical estimate of the *test* error without touching the holdout set. By partitioning the training data into $k$ folds and rotating, we get a robust estimate of how the model will generalize, enabling us to choose the complexity (e.g., polynomial degree, tree depth) that minimizes *estimated* total error.

**(iii) Ensemble Methods (2 marks):** 
- **Bagging** (e.g., Random Forest) trains many models on bootstrapped samples and averages their predictions. Averaging *reduces variance* while leaving bias roughly unchanged. Hence bagging primarily attacks the variance side of the trade-off.
- **Boosting** (e.g., AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting) trains models *sequentially*, with each new model correcting the errors of the previous one. This *reduces bias* by iteratively refining the fit, but it can *increase variance* if over-boosted.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Consider a regression problem with the true function $f(x) = 3x + 2$ and noise $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$. Three models are trained on different samples, yielding predictions at $x_0 = 2$ as $\hat{f}_1 = 7.5$, $\hat{f}_2 = 8.2$, $\hat{f}_3 = 8.9$. Compute the bias, variance, and total expected MSE at $x_0$. Comment on whether the model is overfitting or underfitting. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch and explain the typical curves of (i) training error vs. model complexity, and (ii) test/validation error vs. model complexity. Mark the regions of underfitting, good fit, and overfitting on the diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — True value at $x_0$ (1 mark):**
$$f(2) = 3(2) + 2 = 8$$

**Step 2 — Mean prediction (1 mark):**
$$\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(2)] = \frac{7.5 + 8.2 + 8.9}{3} = \frac{24.6}{3} = 8.2$$

**Step 3 — Bias² (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathrm{Bias}^2 = (8.2 - 8)^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04$$

**Step 4 — Variance (1.5 marks):**
$$\mathrm{Var} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(7.5 - 8.2)^2 + (8.2 - 8.2)^2 + (8.9 - 8.2)^2\right]$$
$$= \frac{1}{3}\left[0.49 + 0 + 0.49\right] = \frac{0.98}{3} \approx 0.327$$

**Step 5 — Total expected MSE (1 mark):**
$$\text{Total} = 0.04 + 0.327 + 1.0 = 1.367$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):** The bias is very small (0.04) while the variance is moderate (0.327). The model is relatively *unbiased* but somewhat *unstable* across training sets, indicating a tendency toward overfitting. The high irreducible error ($1.0$) dominates the total, which is characteristic of noisy data. The fix is to apply regularization or collect more data to reduce variance.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Curve (i) — Training Error vs. Complexity (3 marks):**
Training error *monotonically decreases* as model complexity increases. With a very simple model (e.g., a constant), the training error is high. As complexity grows (e.g., higher-degree polynomial, deeper tree), the model fits the training data more closely, eventually reaching near-zero error for very complex models.

**Curve (ii) — Test/Validation Error vs. Complexity (3 marks):**
Test error has a characteristic **U-shape**. For low-complexity models, both training and test errors are high (underfitting region). As complexity increases, test error decreases, reaches a minimum at the *optimal complexity*, and then begins to rise again as the model overfits (overfitting region). The gap between training and test error widens in the overfitting region.

**Region Marking (1 mark):**
- **Underfitting region:** Left side — both errors high and close together.
- **Good fit region:** Middle — low test error at the U-curve's minimum.
- **Overfitting region:** Right side — training error very low, test error rising.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Pitfall 1 — Forgetting the Square on Bias:** Many students write $\mathrm{Bias} = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - f(x)$ in the decomposition formula. The decomposition uses the *squared* bias. Writing $\mathrm{Bias}$ without the square is a **−1 mark** deduction. **Always write $\mathrm{Bias}^2$.**
> 
> **Pitfall 2 — Confusing Bias with Variance Definitions:** Bias measures the *average* error of the model across training sets; Variance measures the *spread* of the model across training sets. Reversing them in an exam answer costs **2 marks** outright.
> 
> **Pitfall 3 — Saying "Variance is the noise in data":** No. Variance of the *model prediction* is not the same as the noise variance $\sigma^2$. The latter is the irreducible error. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this confusion.
> 
> **Pitfall 4 — Forgetting the Internal Choice Rule:** In KTU 2024 ESE, you must attempt *either* Q.A *or* Q.B from a module. Solving both will NOT give extra marks. Pick the one you know best.
> 
> **Pitfall 5 — Skipping the Derivation Steps:** The 7-mark derivation question in Q.A(a) requires showing the expansion of the square and the disappearance of the cross-term. Writing only the final boxed formula without intermediate steps will fetch at most **3 out of 7 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **expected test MSE** at a point $x$ decomposes into exactly three parts: $\mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- [x] **Bias** = systematic error from model assumptions; **Variance** = sensitivity to training data; $\sigma^2$ = irreducible noise.
- [x] **Increasing complexity** $\Rightarrow$ Bias $\downarrow$, Variance $\uparrow$.
- [x] **Underfitting** = High Bias, Low Variance; **Overfitting** = Low Bias, High Variance.
- [x] **Regularization** trades a small increase in bias for a larger decrease in variance — a deliberate move along the trade-off curve.
- [x] **Bagging** reduces Variance; **Boosting** reduces Bias. Use accordingly.
- [x] The **U-shaped validation error curve** is the empirical signature of the bias-variance trade-off.
- [x] **Cross-validation** is the practitioner's tool for empirically locating the optimal complexity.
- [x] The **irreducible error $\sigma^2$** is a hard floor — no model, however good, can go below it.
- [x] **Derivation rule to memorize:** Add and subtract $\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$, expand the square, use independence of $\varepsilon$ and $\hat{f}$, and recognize variance definition.
- [x] **In the KTU exam:** Always write $\mathrm{Bias}^2$ (squared) and clearly distinguish model variance from noise variance.
- [x] **Numerical problems:** Compute the *mean* prediction first, then bias, then variance, then sum with $\sigma^2$ for the total.
- [x] **Block diagrams** showing the flow of error decomposition earn full credit and are often faster to draw than hand-sketched curves.
- [x] **Choose the simpler model** when two models have similar test error — Occam's razor in disguise (variance reduction is a bonus).
